## Context and Problem Statement

Uniffi currently uses a very coarse locking strategy for managing concurrent access to object instances,
which has caused us to accidentally ship code in a product that [blocked the main thread on network I/O](https://jira.mozilla.com/browse/SDK-157).
We need to enable finer-grained concurrency control in order to provide the desired API for a key consumer.

Currently, every interface has a corresponding [ffi_support::ConcurrentHandleMap](https://docs.rs/ffi-support/0.4.0/ffi_support/handle_map/struct.ConcurrentHandleMap.html) that is responsible for owning all instances of
that interface and for handing out references to them in a mutability-safe and threadsafe manner. This
ensures that the generated code is safe in the face of concurrent operations, but has a substantial
runtime cost: only one method call can be executed on an instance at any time. Any attempt to call an
object method while a concurrent method is already executing, will block until the previous call has completed.

The desired API for Project Nimbus includes methods that will be called synchronously from the main thread,
and hence must not block on network or disk I/O. Such an API cannot be built with uniffi as currently
implemented.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Enabling consumers to control the potential blocking behaviour of their generated APIs.
* Ensure safety of the generated code.
* Ship a solution in a timely manner to unblock Project Nimbus.

## Considered Options

* Option A: Do nothing, require consumers to assume that all method calls might block.
* Option B: Let consumers mark interface definitions as threadsafe to opt in to a less-locking handlemap.
* Option C: Insist that all interfaces be threadsafe and replace the handlemap with raw pointers.
* Option D: Use a less-locking handlemap and rely on calling code to behave safely.
human_decision:
# Let consumers opt out of HandleMap locking if their interface is threadsafe

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: rfkelly, jhugman, mhammond, dmosedale
* Date:  2021-01-13

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option:
 * **Option B: Let consumers mark interface definitions as threadsafe to opt in to a less-locking handlemap.**

The choice here comes down to safety and simplicity. By making a more-concurrency-friendly handlemap
we can maintain the current strict enforcement of Rust's mutability-safety and thread-safety guarantees,
even in the face of errors in the generated bindings. It seems to be a relatively small change, and
by making it opt-in we avoid creating busywork for other consumers who are not urgently facing this
problem.

One downside is that consumers need to opt-in to the fix, meaning that the default behavior may still
be surprising to new consumers. We'll mitigate this with docs and will consider revisiting the default
behaviour if the majority of consumers adopt the new approach.

This choice does also punt some potential performance improvements to future work, but that seems in keeping
with where we are in the project's lifecycle.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Option A: Do nothing, require consumers to assume that all method calls might block.

Make no changes to uniffi, and instead accept the fact that method calls are executed serially.
Document this limitation and work with consumers to update their API definitions to account for it.

* Good, because we can ship this quickly.
* Good, because we don't give up any of the safety guarantees of the current approach.
* Bad, because it makes it basically impossible to meet the needs of one of our key early consumers,
  and would force us into awkward compromises around a suboptimal API.
* Bad, because it makes uniffi less attractive to potential future consumers.
* Bad, because it keeps all the run-time overhead of the handlemap.
* Bad, because the default behaviour still has a hidden mutex, which might be a nasty surprise
  for future consumers in the same way that it was for Nimbus.

Ultimately, we want uniffi to be a tool that helps consumers deliver value, not something that
foist limitations upon them, which makes this option unattractive.

### Option B: Let consumers mark interface definitions as threadsafe to opt in to a less-locking handlemap.

Implement a variant of `ConcurrentHandleMap` that does not protect its members with a Mutex, which
is the main source of constraints on concurrent execution in the current setup. Instead, this handlemap
would only give out immutable references to its members, and would insist that its members are `Send`
and `Sync` so they can be safely accessed from multiple threads.

Introduce a new annotation to the UDL so that `interface` definitions can be declared as threadsafe.
In the generated Rust scaffolding, use the new handlemap for threadsafe interfaces but keep using the
existing `ConcurrentHandleMap` by default.

* Good, because this is a relatively small change from the current behaviour, which should be fairly
  quick to ship.
* Good, because this is a non-breaking change for consumers who don't opt in to it.
* Good, because the `Sync` and `Send` bounds on the underlying struct will help consumers
  to implement their own fine-grained concurrency control while being supported by Rust's
  compile-time guarantees.
* Good, because consumers don't have to care about this until they discover that they need
  fine-grained locking.
* Good, because it leaves the door open to further improvements (like "Option C") in the
  future if we decide that makes sense later.
* Bad, because it keeps all the run-time overhead of the handlemap.
* Bad, because now we need to maintain two handlemap variants.
* Bad, because the default behaviour still has a hidden mutex, which might be a nasty surprise
  for future consumers in the same way that it was for Nimbus.

This is the solution we have ultimately selected.

### Option C: Insist that all interfaces be threadsafe and replace the handlemap with raw pointers.

Stop using `ConcurrentHandleMap` to intermediate object access. Instead, put each object instance
in a `Box` and use `Box::into_raw` to transfer ownership of the box to the foreign language code
as a raw pointer.

Maintain safety by insisting that any struct implementing a UDL `interface` must be `Sync` and `Send`,
and by refusing to hand out mutable references to the boxed instance.

* Good, because it removes the issue as a potential footgun for all consumers.
* Good, because the `Sync` and `Send` bounds on the underlying struct will help consumers
  to implement their own fine-grained concurrency control while being supported by Rust's
  compile-time guarantees.
* Good, because it likely reduces runtime overhead and gives a small performance boost.
* Bad, because it's a breaking change for all consumers, even if they don't care about this issue.
* Bad, because it increases the amount of unsafe rust that we need to emit in the generated scaffolding.
* Bad, because we lose some additional runtime checks provided by the handlemap, such as guarding
  against passing a handle that belongs to a different datatype.
* Bad, because it's a non-trivial departure from the current approach which will take more time to QA.

This seems like a promising longer-term option, especially if we find that the majority of consumers
are opting in to the fix proposed in this ADR. But the additional complexity weights heavily against
trying to ship this as an initial fix for Project Nimbus.

### Option D: Use a less-locking handlemap and rely on calling code to behave safely.

Implement a variant of `ConcurrentHandleMap` that does not protect its members with a Mutex, which
is the main source of constraints on concurrent execution in the current setup. Instead, this handlemap
would hand out references without any runtime checks, on the assumption that the calling code is
behaving in a safe manner. Replace all currently uses of `ConcurrentHandleMap` with this new less-locking variant.

* Good, because this is a relatively small change from the current behaviour, which should be fairly
  quick to ship.
* Bad, because we lost Rust's compile-time safety guarantees.
* Bad, because it's hard to communicate to consumers what "behave safely" really means (and we might not
  even understand it ourselves).

This seems to throw away some of the key safety benefits of using Rust, which makes it a very
unattractive option.

## Implementation Sketch

### Add a `[Threadsafe]` attribute to UDL `interface` definitions.

We would advise Nimbus SDK to update their UDL definition for `NimbusClient` like so:

```idl
[Threadsafe]
interface NimbusClient {
  // existing method definitions remain unchanged
}
```

The [`uniffi_bindgen::interface::Object`](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/blob/803bb3d79daa8ea088fb2d8f05c08ada09821986/uniffi_bindgen/src/interface/mod.rs#L722) struct
would grow a corresponding boolean `threadsafe` field and corresponding public accessor.
When [building an instance of this struct from the UDL](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/blob/803bb3d79daa8ea088fb2d8f05c08ada09821986/uniffi_bindgen/src/interface/mod.rs#L786),
we would inspect the list of attributes to look for one named "Threadsafe" and set the field
if present. The way that we [handle the `[ByRef]` annotation on method arugments](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/blob/803bb3d79daa8ea088fb2d8f05c08ada09821986/uniffi_bindgen/src/interface/mod.rs#L658)
will likely serve as a good example to follow.

### Implement a less-locking HandleMap variant

The `ffi_support` crate provides a basic non-locking [`HandleMap<T>`](https://docs.rs/ffi-support/0.4.0/ffi_support/handle_map/struct.HandleMap.html) struct,
and it implements `ConcurrentHandleMap` as a thin wrapper around a `RwLock<HandleMap<Mutex<T>>>`. We can make our own variant that removes the inner mutex, as a thin wrapper around a`RwLock<HandleMap<Arc<T>>>`. (The outer `RwLock` is still needed in order to guard mutations of the handlemap itself, while the `Arc` is needed so that we can quickly clone a reference and
release the lock before calling potentially-long-running methods on that reference).

Bikeshed name: `LessLockingHandleMap`, implemented under the [`uniffi::ffi` module](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/tree/main/uniffi/src/ffi). It needs to live in the `uniffi` crate so that it can be used by the generated Rust scaffolding at runtime.

We don't need to support the entire `ConcurrentHandleMap` interface, only:
* `insert_with_output`
* `insert_with_result`
* `call_with_output`
* `call_with_result`
* `delete_u64`

The `LessLockingHandleMap<T>` struct should be capable of handing out `&T` references but should never hand
out a `&mut T`. It should also require that `T: Send + Sync`.

### Use `LessLockingHandleMap` in the Rust scaffolding for `[Threadsafe]` instances

In [ObjectTemplate.rs](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/blob/main/uniffi_bindgen/src/templates/ObjectTemplate.rs), check the `threadsafe` property of the object definition. If it's true, use `LessLockingHandleMap` instead of `ConcurrentHandleMap` in the [lazy static that declares the handlemap for that interface](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/blob/803bb3d79daa8ea088fb2d8f05c08ada09821986/uniffi_bindgen/src/templates/ObjectTemplate.rs#L11).

By ensuring that `LessLockingHandleMap` and `ConcurrentHandleMap` expose a similar API, this will hopefully
be a fairly minimal if-then-else that just chooses the correct name of the struct to use.

### ~~Optional: Add a `[Blocking]` annotation to methods and functions~~

(This was moved into a follow-up issue, ref [#378](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/issues/378)).

## Links

* [Fenix Bug: Large regression in MAIN/VIEW start up](https://jira.mozilla.com/browse/SDK-157).
* [An proof-of-concept experiment in using raw pointers rather than a handlemap](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/pull/366).
* [The final implementation of this ADR: #372](https://github.com/mozilla/uniffi-rs/pull/372)
debate_answer: Option B is the preferred choice as it provides a more controlled and safer path to achieving the desired non-blocking behavior, respecting the diversity of consumer needs and capabilities.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for Option B, emphasizing consumer flexibility, safety, and incremental adoption. This approach allows consumers to opt-in to a less-locking strategy, maintaining safety for those who do not need it while providing a timely solution for Project Nimbus. It also encourages best practices in concurrency management, aligning well with the decision drivers.
message_history:
In evaluating the options, we need to consider the balance between safety, performance, and the ability to meet the requirements of Project Nimbus in a timely manner.

**Option A: Do nothing, require consumers to assume that all method calls might block.**
- Pros: No changes needed, maintaining current safety guarantees.
- Cons: Does not address the problem of blocking the main thread, which is a critical requirement for Project Nimbus. This option is not viable if we want to meet the project's needs.

**Option B: Let consumers mark interface definitions as threadsafe to opt in to a less-locking handlemap.**
- Pros: Provides flexibility to consumers, allowing them to choose when to use a less-locking strategy. This could lead to better performance for those who opt-in.
- Cons: Places the burden on consumers to understand and correctly implement threadsafe interfaces, which could lead to errors and safety issues. It might also delay the solution as consumers need to make changes.

**Option C: Insist that all interfaces be threadsafe and replace the handlemap with raw pointers.**
- Pros: Could potentially offer the best performance by eliminating the handlemap overhead.
- Cons: Significant risk to safety, as raw pointers can lead to undefined behavior if not managed correctly. This option could introduce more bugs and is not advisable without strong guarantees of safety.

**Option D: Use a less-locking handlemap and rely on calling code to behave safely.**
- Pros: Reduces blocking without requiring consumers to make changes, potentially offering a good balance between performance and ease of use. It allows for a more immediate solution to unblock Project Nimbus.
- Cons: Relies on the calling code to behave safely, which could lead to concurrency issues if not handled properly. However, this risk might be mitigated with proper documentation and guidelines.

Given the context and decision drivers, **Option D** seems to be the most balanced choice. It addresses the immediate need to reduce blocking for Project Nimbus while maintaining a level of safety by still using a handlemap, albeit a less-locking one. It also does not require consumers to make immediate changes, which aligns with the goal of shipping a solution in a timely manner. Proper documentation and guidelines can help mitigate the risks associated with relying on calling code to behave safely.
While Option D offers a balanced approach, I would argue that **Option B** might be a more suitable choice given the context and decision drivers. Here's why:

**Option B: Let consumers mark interface definitions as threadsafe to opt in to a less-locking handlemap.**

1. **Consumer Flexibility and Control**: This option empowers consumers by giving them the choice to opt into a less-locking strategy. This means that those who need non-blocking behavior, like Project Nimbus, can achieve it without forcing all consumers to adopt a potentially riskier concurrency model.

2. **Safety Considerations**: By allowing consumers to mark interfaces as threadsafe, we maintain a higher level of safety compared to Option D. Consumers who opt-in are likely to have the expertise or specific use cases that justify the trade-off between safety and performance.

3. **Incremental Adoption**: This approach allows for incremental adoption. Consumers can gradually transition to a less-locking model as they become more comfortable with the implications, reducing the risk of widespread concurrency issues.

4. **Timely Solution**: While it requires some changes from consumers, the option can still be implemented in a timely manner for those who need it urgently, like Project Nimbus. It also encourages best practices in concurrency management by making threadsafety an explicit choice.

5. **Documentation and Support**: With proper documentation and support, consumers can be guided on how to safely implement threadsafe interfaces, mitigating potential risks.

In summary, Option B provides a more controlled and safer path to achieving the desired non-blocking behavior. It respects the diversity of consumer needs and capabilities, allowing those who require advanced concurrency control to opt-in while maintaining the current safety guarantees for others. This approach aligns well with the decision drivers by enabling consumer control, ensuring safety, and providing a timely solution for Project Nimbus.

## Context and Problem Statement

Payment data used in Stripe, PayPal, Apple, and Google IAP are handled as individual documents. We typically need to access different portions of these at different times. In addition, their schema occasionally changes (API updates). Stripe and PayPal have API limits, which means we typically need to duplicate/cache data we retrieve from them. At the moment we cache this data in Redis. This mostly works, but restricts our ability to run queries across payment data sets without pulling it all into BigQuery, or making substantial amounts of Stripe/PayPal requests.

To handle IAP, we need to store Apple receipts and Google subscription objects. These should be organized with the FxA user they belong to. They're arbitrary JSON documents that we also need copied to BigQuery for ETL jobs. To future-proof our payment data storage needs we should have the flexibility needed to store Stripe/Paypal payment data in the same data store with the same ETL capability.

## Decision Drivers

- Engineering resources
- Arbitrary JSON document querying ability
- Ease of loading data for ETL jobs
- Capable of storing arbitrary JSON data
- Integration with Stripe and other payment systems

## Considered Options

- Google Firestore
- MySQL
- Redis
human_decision:
# Store Payment Data in Firestore

- Deciders: Ben Bangert, Barry Chen, Bianca Danforth, Danny Coates, Les Orchard
- Date: 2021-07-22

## Decision Outcome

Google Firestore. We've used Firestore in the event-broker, it has a rich API, and extensions that overlap with our requirements that reduces engineering and operations resources.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Google Firestore

Firestore is a JSON document oriented datastore that stores documents in collections (tables). It also has the ability to nest collections under documents, so a single user document could have its own collection of payment documents. Because it has no defined schema, arbitrary JSON documents can be stored.

Firestore has been used for event-broker, along with its streaming watch capability for immediate updates to cached data used to dispatch events. Stripe has built multiple extensions for Firestore that keep invoice and customer data in sync. Google has also built a variety of extensions for Firestore including a BigQuery sync extension that would handle our ETL needs.

Pros:

- We already use Firestore, its integrated in fxa-event-broker.
- Operationally simple, Google hosts and manages it.
- Firestore Extensions with supported Stripe and BigQuery extensions handle requirements for us.
- Comprehensive querying capability on any portion of the JSON document.
- Document nesting for organization of payment data.

Cons:

- May still need to cache commonly accessed data in Redis to reduce Firestore spend.
- Increases databases that auth-server talks with.

### MySQL

Firefox Accounts uses MySQL for most of its data. Some short-lived re-creatable data is stored only in Redis (OAuth token related). We could store payment data in MySQL with the rest of the user data. MySQL supports JSON documents and has some limited query capability of them. MySQL 8 has additional JSON functionality for partial document updates that would be required for our use.

Pros:

- We already use MySQL, its integrated in fxa-auth-server.
- No additional setup.

Cons:

- Can't query by arbitrary paths into the JSON document.
- We aren't using MySQL 8, which complicates JSON document manipulation.

### Redis

Firefox Accounts uses Redis heavily for caching and short term data storage. It's fast, and we use it for caching a variety of JSON documents already.

Pros:

- Already integrated in auth-server.
- Fast with little additional cost/resource overhead to use it more.

Cons:

- We aren't using Redis in a cluster mode that ensures data durability.
- No query ability over portions of a document, as its stored as a string.
debate_answer: Google Firestore is the best option for storing and managing payment data from Stripe, PayPal, Apple, and Google IAP, given its strengths in handling arbitrary JSON documents, integration with Google Cloud services, and scalability.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for Google Firestore, emphasizing its ability to handle arbitrary JSON documents, integration with Google Cloud services, and scalability. These features align well with the requirements of handling payment data from multiple sources with changing schemas. While the negative side highlights MySQL's familiarity and complex querying capabilities, it does not address the need for flexibility in handling JSON documents and seamless integration with BigQuery as effectively as Firestore does.
message_history:
In evaluating the best option for storing and managing payment data from Stripe, PayPal, Apple, and Google IAP, we need to consider the decision drivers and the specific requirements of the system. Let's analyze each of the considered options:

1. **Google Firestore**:
   - **Pros**:
     - Firestore is a NoSQL document database that is designed to store and query arbitrary JSON documents, which aligns well with the need to handle changing schemas and arbitrary JSON data.
     - It offers strong integration with Google Cloud services, making it easier to load data into BigQuery for ETL jobs.
     - Firestore provides real-time data synchronization and offline support, which can be beneficial for applications that require real-time updates.
     - It scales automatically and can handle large volumes of data, which is useful given the potential size of payment data.
   - **Cons**:
     - It may require more engineering resources to set up and manage compared to Redis, especially if the team is not already familiar with Google Cloud services.
     - While Firestore is good for querying JSON documents, complex queries might require additional indexing or data structuring.

2. **MySQL**:
   - **Pros**:
     - MySQL is a well-known relational database that many engineers are familiar with, potentially reducing the learning curve.
     - It can handle complex queries and joins, which might be useful for certain types of data analysis.
   - **Cons**:
     - MySQL is not inherently designed for storing arbitrary JSON documents, although it does support JSON data types. This might lead to challenges in handling schema changes and arbitrary data structures.
     - It may not integrate as seamlessly with BigQuery for ETL processes compared to Firestore.
     - Scaling MySQL to handle large volumes of data can be more complex and resource-intensive.

3. **Redis**:
   - **Pros**:
     - Redis is an in-memory data store known for its speed, which is beneficial for caching and quick data retrieval.
     - It is relatively easy to set up and manage, especially for caching purposes.
   - **Cons**:
     - Redis is not designed for long-term storage of large datasets or complex querying, which limits its usefulness for the described use case.
     - It lacks native support for querying arbitrary JSON documents and might require additional tools or workarounds to achieve this.
     - Integration with BigQuery for ETL jobs is not as straightforward as with Firestore.

**Conclusion**:
Given the need to store arbitrary JSON documents, handle schema changes, and integrate with BigQuery for ETL jobs, **Google Firestore** appears to be the best option. It provides the necessary flexibility for storing and querying JSON data, integrates well with Google Cloud services, and can scale to meet future data storage needs. While it may require some initial investment in terms of engineering resources, its benefits in terms of functionality and integration make it a strong choice for this use case.
While Google Firestore offers several advantages, I would argue that **MySQL** could be a viable option worth considering, especially if the team has existing expertise with relational databases. Here's why:

1. **Familiarity and Resource Efficiency**:
   - Many engineering teams are already familiar with MySQL, which can reduce the learning curve and the need for additional training. This familiarity can lead to more efficient use of engineering resources, as the team can leverage existing knowledge and tools.

2. **Complex Querying Capabilities**:
   - MySQL excels at handling complex queries and joins, which can be beneficial for analyzing payment data across different dimensions. While it may not be as flexible as Firestore for JSON documents, MySQL's ability to perform complex queries can be a significant advantage for certain types of data analysis.

3. **JSON Support**:
   - Although MySQL is a relational database, it does support JSON data types. This allows for the storage and querying of JSON documents, albeit with some limitations compared to a NoSQL database. For many use cases, MySQL's JSON support may be sufficient, especially if the JSON data structure is relatively stable.

4. **Integration with ETL Processes**:
   - While Firestore integrates well with BigQuery, MySQL can also be integrated into ETL processes using various tools and connectors. The choice of ETL tools can mitigate some of the integration challenges, allowing for efficient data transfer to BigQuery or other analytics platforms.

5. **Scalability and Performance**:
   - MySQL has matured significantly in terms of scalability and performance, especially with the advent of cloud-based managed MySQL services. These services can handle large volumes of data and provide automated scaling, backups, and maintenance.

6. **Cost Considerations**:
   - Depending on the specific use case and data volume, MySQL might offer a more cost-effective solution compared to Firestore, especially if the team can leverage existing infrastructure and expertise.

**Conclusion**:
While Google Firestore is a strong candidate due to its flexibility and integration capabilities, MySQL should not be dismissed outright. Its strengths in complex querying, familiarity, and potential cost-effectiveness make it a viable option, particularly if the JSON data structure is manageable within a relational framework. Ultimately, the decision should consider the team's expertise, the complexity of the data, and the specific requirements of the ETL processes.

topic: ## Context and Problem Statement Given a service is using the twirl template imports recommended by the play-frontend libraries. When a developer tries to use an import with a name which is present in both libraries without explicitly importing the one they want. Then they will get a compilation error: Currently, we recommend the use of the wildcard twirl imports, but this possible consequence is undocumented behaviour. The compilation error can be resolved by explicitly importing the class they want. ## Decision Drivers * Be unsurprising for developers (examples we give should be runnable without triggering an exception) * As much as possible avoid breaking changes for existing services ## Considered Options * **Option 1:** Documentation changes 1. Add a note about the issue and explain how to resolve so that developers who run into this can unblock themselves * Will developers be able to find this easily enough when they need it? 2. Stop recommending twirl key imports and instead ask for developers to use explicit imports * Will this have a negative impact on use of implicit helpers which may no longer be easily discoverable by developers? * Keep suggestion to wildcard import implicits, but not via twirl keys, do it in the template * Keep documentation about how they could use wildcard imports but with caveat of risk of collision * **Option2:** API changes 1. Do nothing 2. Rename view models to make them unique between libraries * Will it actually be possible to add “Hmrc” or “Govuk” to the front of a view model or will that conflict with other classes in the libraries? * Our twirl components in views.html.components are compiled to classes with a lower case leading letter, like hmrcCharacterCount so we could rename our view models to HmrcCharacterCount, however we do currently alias HmrcCharacterCount to hmrcCharacterCount in components/package.scala. If teams import this rather than the lower case version then it’s going to be an ambiguous import and break when you try to compile it. * If it’s not possible, what else could we consider? * Rename with a new suffix like VM or ViewModel * And also adding the prefix to match the component? * Would we rename all the view models, or just those that conflict? * What would the impact of this be on services? 3. Is it possible if both libraries are imported to prefer / override / exclude the govuk version when the hmrc is included - to include both via hmrc and omit the conflicted govuk versions

# Resolve naming collisions between libraries

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: platui
* Date: 2021-08-02

Technical Story: PLATUI-1237 (and related to this is PLATUI-1294)

## Decision Outcome

We've decided to immediately implement Option 1.2 and defer further changes to be discussed as part of PLATUI-1294.

### Positive Consequences

* Less confusing for new developers where imports are coming from when we avoid recommending using TwirlKeys config. 
* Developers can see as part of our recommended usage instructions the possibility for an ambiguous import exception and
  what they can do to resolve them without.

### Negative Consequences

* Without an API change the naming of components and view models is not as intuitive as we think it could be which may
  confuse new developers.
debate_answer: Option 2: API changes, specifically renaming view models to make them unique, is the recommended solution.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for addressing the root cause of the issue by making API changes. While this approach involves more initial effort and potential breaking changes, it offers a permanent solution that improves the developer experience and future-proofs the system. The affirmative side's suggestion of documentation changes provides a quick fix but does not resolve the underlying problem, leading to potential ongoing issues. Therefore, the long-term benefits of Option 2 outweigh the short-term challenges.
message_history:
In evaluating the best option to address the issue of ambiguous imports in the twirl template system, we need to consider the decision drivers: being unsurprising for developers and avoiding breaking changes for existing services.

**Option 1: Documentation Changes**

1. **Pros:**
   - **Immediate Guidance:** By adding documentation, developers who encounter the issue can quickly find a solution. This is a low-cost and immediate fix.
   - **Minimal Disruption:** It does not require any changes to the existing codebase or APIs, thus avoiding breaking changes.
   - **Flexibility:** Developers can choose to continue using wildcard imports with an understanding of the potential risks, or switch to explicit imports if they prefer.

2. **Cons:**
   - **Discoverability:** There is a risk that developers may not find the documentation easily, especially if they are new to the system or the issue.
   - **Partial Solution:** It addresses the symptom (compilation error) rather than the root cause (naming conflicts).

**Option 2: API Changes**

1. **Pros:**
   - **Long-term Solution:** Renaming view models to make them unique would eliminate the ambiguity, providing a more robust solution.
   - **Clarity:** It would make the codebase clearer and reduce the likelihood of similar issues in the future.

2. **Cons:**
   - **Breaking Changes:** Renaming view models could break existing services that rely on the current naming conventions, requiring significant refactoring.
   - **Complexity:** Implementing changes to prefer or exclude certain versions of imports could add complexity to the system and might not be feasible.

**Conclusion:**

Given the decision drivers, Option 1 seems to be the more prudent choice. It provides an immediate, low-impact solution that helps developers without introducing breaking changes. While it does not address the root cause, it allows for a smoother transition and gives time to consider a more comprehensive solution if needed. 

Option 2, while potentially more effective in the long term, carries a higher risk of disruption and complexity. It might be worth considering as a future enhancement, but only after careful planning and communication with developers to mitigate the impact on existing services. 

Therefore, I would recommend starting with Option 1, focusing on improving documentation and guidance for developers, while keeping Option 2 as a potential future consideration.
While I understand the rationale behind choosing Option 1, I would argue that Option 2, specifically renaming view models to make them unique, is the better choice despite its challenges. Here's why:

**Option 2: API Changes**

1. **Pros:**
   - **Permanent Resolution:** By addressing the root cause of the issue—naming conflicts—Option 2 provides a permanent solution. This eliminates the need for developers to constantly refer to documentation to resolve ambiguities.
   - **Improved Developer Experience:** A clear and unambiguous API is more intuitive and less error-prone, leading to a better developer experience in the long run.
   - **Future-Proofing:** As the codebase evolves, having unique identifiers will prevent similar issues from arising, making the system more robust.

2. **Cons:**
   - **Breaking Changes:** While renaming view models could introduce breaking changes, these can be managed through a well-planned deprecation strategy. Providing aliases or transitional support can help mitigate the impact on existing services.
   - **Initial Complexity:** The initial effort to implement these changes is higher, but the long-term benefits of a cleaner and more maintainable codebase outweigh the short-term complexity.

**Conclusion:**

While Option 1 offers a quick fix, it does not solve the underlying problem and relies on developers to be aware of and find the documentation when needed. This can lead to repeated issues and frustration, especially for new developers.

Option 2, although more challenging to implement, provides a comprehensive solution that aligns with the decision drivers of being unsurprising for developers and avoiding future breaking changes. By investing in a one-time change to make view models unique, we can create a more predictable and stable environment for developers.

Therefore, I recommend pursuing Option 2 with careful planning and communication to manage the transition effectively. This approach not only resolves the current issue but also enhances the overall quality and usability of the system.

## Context and Problem Statement <!-- required -->

Fedora 3 is unsupported and has been unsupported for four years; it is unlikely to be supported in the foreseeable future. It also requires a difficult-to-support version of the Java Virtual Machine. And yet, Fedora 3 is the cornerstone of our management "repository," in which all SDR content is managed and from which said content flows to access and preservation environments. At the same time, there is a dwindling number of organizations in the cultural heritage community who are still using Fedora 3.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Fedora 3 is unsupported and unlikely to be supported
* Fedora 3 will be harder to install on newer operating systems
* The Fedora 3 data model is not inherently validatable
* The Fedora 3 community is disappearing, so we are increasingly going it alone
* Fedora 3 is a critical piece of SDR infrastructure and represents an enormous risk
* Samvera software that supports Fedora 3 is outdated and maintained/supported only through our own efforts, preventing us from using mainstream Samvera software

## Considered Options <!-- required -->

* Do nothing
* Keep using Fedora 3 & DLSS Infrastructure Team takes over maintenance of Fedora 3 and Fedora 3-supporting software in Samvera community
* Replace Fedora 3 with PostgreSQL
* Replace Fedora 3 with latest Fedora version

debate_answer: Replace Fedora 3 with PostgreSQL.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for transitioning to PostgreSQL, emphasizing flexibility, broad adoption, and independence from specific software ecosystems. These factors provide a more future-proof and adaptable solution compared to upgrading to the latest Fedora version, which still ties the organization to a specific platform. PostgreSQL's strong data integrity features and potential for cost savings further strengthen the case for this option.
message_history:
Given the context and problem statement, the best option would be to replace Fedora 3 with the latest Fedora version. Here's why:

1. **Long-term Viability**: Fedora 3 is unsupported and unlikely to receive support in the future. Continuing to use it poses a significant risk to the sustainability and security of the SDR infrastructure. By upgrading to the latest Fedora version, you align with a supported and actively developed platform, ensuring long-term viability.

2. **Community and Support**: The Fedora 3 community is dwindling, which means less support and fewer resources available for troubleshooting and development. The latest Fedora version would have a more active community, providing better support and collaboration opportunities.

3. **Compatibility and Modernization**: Newer Fedora versions are likely to be more compatible with modern operating systems and technologies. This reduces the complexity and cost associated with maintaining outdated systems and ensures that the infrastructure can evolve with technological advancements.

4. **Data Model and Validation**: The Fedora 3 data model is not inherently validatable, which can lead to data integrity issues. Newer versions of Fedora may offer improved data models and validation mechanisms, enhancing the reliability and accuracy of the repository.

5. **Integration with Samvera**: The outdated Samvera software supporting Fedora 3 limits the ability to use mainstream Samvera software. Upgrading to the latest Fedora version would likely improve integration with Samvera, allowing for better use of its features and community support.

While replacing Fedora 3 with PostgreSQL could be considered, it would require significant changes to the data model and architecture, which might not be feasible or desirable given the current reliance on Fedora's specific features. Therefore, upgrading to the latest Fedora version is a more balanced approach, addressing the immediate risks while providing a path forward for future development and support.
While upgrading to the latest Fedora version seems like a logical step, I would argue that replacing Fedora 3 with PostgreSQL might be a more strategic choice for the following reasons:

1. **Flexibility and Customization**: PostgreSQL is a highly flexible and powerful relational database system that allows for extensive customization. By transitioning to PostgreSQL, you can design a data model that precisely fits your needs, potentially improving performance and scalability.

2. **Broad Adoption and Support**: PostgreSQL is widely adopted across various industries, ensuring a robust community and extensive support resources. This broad adoption means that there are numerous tools, libraries, and expertise available, which can facilitate the transition and ongoing maintenance.

3. **Independence from Specific Software Ecosystems**: By moving away from a Fedora-specific solution, you reduce dependency on a single software ecosystem. This independence can be beneficial in the long run, as it allows for greater flexibility in choosing tools and technologies that best meet your needs.

4. **Data Integrity and Validation**: PostgreSQL offers strong data integrity features, including ACID compliance and advanced validation capabilities. This can address the current issues with the Fedora 3 data model not being inherently validatable, ensuring more reliable data management.

5. **Cost and Resource Efficiency**: While the initial transition to PostgreSQL might require significant effort, it could lead to cost savings in the long term. PostgreSQL's efficiency and the availability of skilled professionals can reduce the burden on your team and lower maintenance costs.

6. **Future-Proofing**: As technology evolves, having a flexible and widely supported database system like PostgreSQL can make it easier to adapt to future changes and integrate with new technologies.

While upgrading to the latest Fedora version might seem like a straightforward path, it still ties you to a specific platform with its own set of limitations and dependencies. Transitioning to PostgreSQL, although initially challenging, could provide a more robust, flexible, and future-proof solution for managing your SDR content.

## Context and Problem Statement

We want to have telemetry (performance, monitoring) and log collections for each MICO service that should be added automatically during deployment.

## Decision Drivers

* MUST be compatible with Kubernetes
* MUST at least provide all required features (telemetry, logs)
* MUST be independent of the technology used in the respective service
* SHOULD consume less resources

## Considered Options

* Istio
* Envoy
human_decision:
# Istio vs. Envoy

Part of the Technical Story: [Evaluate Istio vs. Envoy vs. xxx](https://github.com/UST-MICO/mico/issues/75)

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: Istio, because it is easier to configure than only using Envoy. By using Istio the Envoy proxies can be used as the data plane and Istio as the control plane to manage the whole service mesh.
Moreover Istio is the better choice for the future, because it has all features that could be potentially helpful for the management of a service mesh.

### Positive Consequences

* Istio enables more than just having telemetry and log collections. In addition it provides many more features that could be useful for MICO in future (follow-up decisions required).

  [Examples](https://istio.io/docs/concepts/what-is-istio/#why-use-istio):
  + Automatic Load Balancing
  + Routing rules, retries, failovers, and fault injection
  + Pluggable policy layer with access controls, rate limits and quotas
  + Secure service-to-service communication in the cluster

### Negative consequences

* Istio consumes many resources
* Istio brings more complexity into the MICO system

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Istio

* Good, because it provides the control plane to configure the Envoy proxies (sidecars)
  + Istio Proxy (extended version of Envoy) is easier to configure than plain Envoy
  + It is integrated into Kubernetes with Custom Resource Definitions, therefore Istio is configurable with the same YAML DSL than Kubernetes
* Good, because it provides additional functionality that could be relevant in future (e.g. access control, secure communication, routing rules)
* Bad, because Istio has a steep learning curve to get familiar with all its features
* Bad, because it is still in development (though the current version is 1.0.5)

### Envoy

* Good, because it is a high-performance proxy with a lightweight footprint (developed in C++)
* Good, because it a graduated project of the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (next to Kubernetes and Prometheus)
* Bad, because the YAML DSL for configuration of Envoy differs from that of Kubernetes, which we are accustomed to use
* Bad, because in a service mesh it's complex and error prone to configure
debate_answer: Envoy is the better choice for automatically adding telemetry and log collections during the deployment of MICO services, given its resource efficiency and flexibility.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for Envoy based on resource efficiency, flexibility, and simplicity, which align well with the decision drivers, particularly the requirement to consume fewer resources. Envoy's lightweight nature and modularity make it a suitable choice for environments where resource constraints and performance are critical factors.
message_history:
When considering the options of Istio and Envoy for automatically adding telemetry and log collections during the deployment of MICO services, we need to evaluate them against the decision drivers provided.

1. **Compatibility with Kubernetes**: Both Istio and Envoy are compatible with Kubernetes. Istio is a service mesh that runs on Kubernetes, and Envoy is a proxy that can be used within a Kubernetes environment. Therefore, both options meet this requirement.

2. **Providing Required Features (Telemetry, Logs)**: 
   - **Istio**: Istio provides a comprehensive set of features for telemetry and logging. It includes built-in support for monitoring, tracing, and logging, and integrates well with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger. Istio's service mesh capabilities allow for detailed insights into service behavior and performance.
   - **Envoy**: Envoy is a high-performance proxy that can be used to collect telemetry and logs. While it provides the necessary features, it often requires additional configuration or integration with other tools to achieve the same level of functionality as Istio.

3. **Independence from Technology Used in the Service**: 
   - **Istio**: Istio abstracts the underlying technology of the services, providing a uniform way to manage telemetry and logs across different services, regardless of the technology stack.
   - **Envoy**: Envoy also operates independently of the service technology, but it might require more manual setup to achieve the same level of abstraction as Istio.

4. **Resource Consumption**:
   - **Istio**: Istio is known to be resource-intensive due to its comprehensive feature set. It can add significant overhead to the system, which might be a concern if resource consumption is a critical factor.
   - **Envoy**: Envoy, being a lightweight proxy, generally consumes fewer resources compared to Istio. It can be a more efficient choice if minimizing resource usage is a priority.

**Conclusion**: 

- If the primary concern is having a robust, out-of-the-box solution for telemetry and logging with minimal configuration, Istio is the better choice. It provides a comprehensive set of features and integrates well with existing monitoring tools, making it easier to manage and deploy.
  
- However, if resource consumption is a significant concern and you are willing to invest in additional configuration to achieve the desired level of telemetry and logging, Envoy might be the more efficient option.

Ultimately, the decision may depend on the specific priorities and constraints of your deployment environment. If ease of use and feature completeness are more important, Istio is likely the better choice. If resource efficiency is paramount, Envoy could be more suitable.
I understand your perspective, but I would argue that Envoy might be the better choice for the following reasons:

1. **Resource Efficiency**: One of the decision drivers is that the solution should consume fewer resources. Envoy, being a lightweight proxy, is generally more resource-efficient than Istio. This is particularly important in environments where resource constraints are a significant concern. By choosing Envoy, you can achieve the necessary telemetry and logging capabilities without the additional overhead that Istio might introduce.

2. **Flexibility and Modularity**: While Istio provides a comprehensive set of features, it can be seen as a more monolithic solution. Envoy, on the other hand, offers flexibility and modularity, allowing you to integrate only the components you need. This can lead to a more tailored and efficient setup, especially if you are willing to invest in configuring and integrating additional tools to achieve the desired functionality.

3. **Independence and Simplicity**: Although Istio abstracts the underlying technology, it also introduces complexity due to its extensive feature set. Envoy, being a simpler and more focused tool, can provide the necessary independence from the service technology without the added complexity. This simplicity can be advantageous in environments where ease of maintenance and straightforward configurations are preferred.

4. **Scalability and Performance**: Envoy's lightweight nature can lead to better performance and scalability, especially in high-traffic environments. By minimizing resource consumption, Envoy can help ensure that the system remains responsive and efficient as the number of services and the volume of traffic increase.

In conclusion, while Istio offers a robust and feature-rich solution, Envoy's resource efficiency, flexibility, and simplicity make it a compelling choice, particularly when resource consumption and performance are critical factors. If you are prepared to handle some additional configuration, Envoy can provide a more efficient and scalable solution for telemetry and logging in a Kubernetes environment.

## Context and Problem Statement

Analysis and visualization of experiment result data needs to be [part of the experiment console](https://github.com/mozilla/nimbus-shared/blob/main/docs/adr/0001-one-console-app.md). The mockups based on user interviews are [here](https://www.figma.com/file/5SLa07XqQb0ttB1ay1gami/Nimbus-Console-UI).

Some decisions need to be made about how the data will be pulled out of [Jetstream](https://github.com/mozilla/jetstream) and visualized within the console. This ADR will highlight how this will be done.


## Decision Drivers

Drivers for fetching data:
* Data is easy to fetch and minimizes the amount of new state added

Drivers for visualizing data:
* Reuse existing [Graph Paper](https://github.com/graph-paper-org/graph-paper) UI components if possible
* How easy or possible is it to integrate Svelte components into React (since Graph Paper is in Svelte)


## Considered Options

**Fetching Data**
1. Add a POST endpoint to Experiment Console to send data over from Jetstream. This data would be saved in the Django database and fetched from the front-end when needed using GraphQL.
2. The visualization front end fetches data via a Django REST API, which fetches Jetstream data on demand using GCS dependencies. Data is not stored in the database.

**Frontend**
1. Use nvd3 or other graphing library as an alternative to Graph Paper
2. Use Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs, otherwise, all visualizations from the mockup will be in React
3. Use Svelte for the entire visualization portion of the console
human_decision:
# Analysis Visualization: Architecture Approach

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: Marina Samuel, Jared Lockhart, Kate Hudson
* Date: 2020-09-16

## Decision Outcome

**Fetching Data**
Option (2) was chosen.

While option (2) requires adding more state such as bucket credentials, it is more straight-forward than option (1). It does not require any changes on the Jetstream side to make POST calls. It also does not require storing the data to the Django database. This is ok since the aggregated data is very small, in the range of around 100kb per fetch. Additionally, giving write access to Jetstream would also change the authentication requirements for it and may require a security review.

It is worth noting that there will be no caching layer to start since the response seems to be fairly quick. However, in the future, if we start sending more data or observe any slowness, we can consider adding cachine.

**Frontend**
Option (2) was chosen. At a high level, this would look like:
1. From within the React application, per experiment result page, in one REST API call, send the experiment slug.
2. Receive all visualization data for that experiment (will include multiple graphs).
3. Take that data and feed it into already-existing Svlete components for display
Note that the existing Graph Paper components are not comprehensive, so some additional code will be written in Svelte to piece together and use the Graph Paper components.

Option (1) is not ideal since existing libraries would be less customizable than our in-house library that we have already invested in.  The only reason to have chosen this was if it was not possible to embed Svelte in React. However, this has been proven to be possible. The method that has been shown to work looks like:
1. Add init() function hooks in the Svelte code where data and a DOM node can be passed in.
2. Create a bundle.js for the Svelte package
3. Include the bundle in the React code and call the init() function, passing in a DOM node to render in and data required for graphs.

Option (3) has the benefit of keeping the entire visualization component separated and written in the same language. The main issue with this is that, given the mockups, some portions of the mockups may be reused in other parts of the console (e.g. the overview table). So they should be available in React.
Furthermore, the only reason for using Svelte is for graphing purposes. It would be best to stick with same conventions/standard of using React wherever possible within the codebase.
debate_answer: Fetching Data: Option 2 - Fetch data via a Django REST API on demand. Frontend Visualization: Option 2 - Use Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs, otherwise, all visualizations in React.
reason: The affirmative side provides a solution that aligns with the decision drivers of minimizing new state and reusing existing components. Fetching data on demand simplifies the architecture and reduces the need for database management, while using Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs allows for component reuse and maintains the majority of the console in React, which is likely more aligned with existing infrastructure.
message_history:
To select the best options for both data fetching and frontend visualization, we need to consider the decision drivers and the context provided.

### Fetching Data

**Option 1: Add a POST endpoint to Experiment Console**

- **Pros:**
  - Data is stored in the Django database, which can be beneficial for caching and historical data analysis.
  - GraphQL can provide a flexible and efficient way to query the data.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Increases the amount of state that needs to be managed in the database.
  - Potentially more complex to implement and maintain due to the need for data synchronization between Jetstream and the database.

**Option 2: Fetch data via a Django REST API on demand**

- **Pros:**
  - Minimizes the amount of new state added, as data is fetched on demand and not stored.
  - Simpler architecture with fewer components to manage.
  
- **Cons:**
  - May result in slower data retrieval times if data needs to be fetched from Jetstream each time.
  - Potentially less efficient if the same data is requested frequently.

**Recommendation:**
Option 2 is preferable for fetching data because it aligns with the decision driver of minimizing the amount of new state added. It also simplifies the architecture by avoiding the need to store and manage data in the database.

### Frontend Visualization

**Option 1: Use nvd3 or other graphing library**

- **Pros:**
  - Provides flexibility in choosing a library that best fits the needs.
  - Potentially easier integration with React if the library is React-friendly.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Does not leverage existing Graph Paper components, which may lead to more development work.

**Option 2: Use Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs, otherwise, all visualizations in React**

- **Pros:**
  - Reuses existing Graph Paper components, aligning with the decision driver.
  - Limits the use of Svelte to only where necessary, reducing integration complexity.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Requires integration between Svelte and React, which may introduce some complexity.

**Option 3: Use Svelte for the entire visualization portion**

- **Pros:**
  - Consistent use of Svelte for all visualizations, potentially simplifying the development process.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Requires a full commitment to Svelte, which may not align with existing React-based components and infrastructure.
  - May require more effort to integrate with the rest of the React-based console.

**Recommendation:**
Option 2 is the best choice for frontend visualization. It allows for the reuse of existing Graph Paper components, which is a key decision driver, while also maintaining the majority of the console in React, which is likely more aligned with the existing infrastructure.

### Conclusion

- **Fetching Data:** Option 2 - Fetch data via a Django REST API on demand.
- **Frontend Visualization:** Option 2 - Use Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs, otherwise, all visualizations in React.
I understand your perspective, but I would like to present an alternative viewpoint for consideration.

### Fetching Data

**Option 1: Add a POST endpoint to Experiment Console**

- **Pros:**
  - Storing data in the Django database allows for efficient querying and analysis over time, which can be crucial for understanding trends and making data-driven decisions.
  - GraphQL's flexibility in querying can lead to more efficient data retrieval, especially when dealing with complex data structures.

- **Cons:**
  - While it does increase the state to be managed, this can be mitigated with proper database management and caching strategies.
  - The complexity of synchronization can be addressed with robust data pipelines and monitoring.

**Option 2: Fetch data via a Django REST API on demand**

- **Pros:**
  - Simplifies the architecture by not storing data, which can be beneficial for reducing overhead.
  
- **Cons:**
  - On-demand fetching can lead to latency issues, especially if the data source (Jetstream) is not optimized for frequent access.
  - Frequent data requests can lead to increased load on Jetstream, potentially affecting performance.

**Recommendation:**
Option 1 might be more suitable for fetching data if the goal is to have a robust system that supports historical data analysis and efficient querying. The added complexity can be managed with proper infrastructure and practices.

### Frontend Visualization

**Option 1: Use nvd3 or other graphing library**

- **Pros:**
  - Offers flexibility and potentially better integration with React, which is the primary framework for the console.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Does not leverage existing Graph Paper components, which could lead to increased development time.

**Option 2: Use Graph Paper/Svelte only for graphs, otherwise, all visualizations in React**

- **Pros:**
  - Reuses existing components, which is efficient and aligns with the decision drivers.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Integration between Svelte and React can be complex and may introduce maintenance challenges.

**Option 3: Use Svelte for the entire visualization portion**

- **Pros:**
  - Consistent use of Svelte can lead to a more cohesive visualization experience.
  
- **Cons:**
  - Requires a shift from React, which may not be feasible given existing infrastructure and expertise.

**Recommendation:**
Option 1 could be considered for frontend visualization if the primary goal is to maintain consistency with React and leverage libraries that integrate seamlessly with it. This approach can reduce complexity and align with existing development practices.

### Conclusion

- **Fetching Data:** Option 1 - Add a POST endpoint to Experiment Console to store data in the Django database.
- **Frontend Visualization:** Option 1 - Use nvd3 or other graphing library for better integration with React.

This approach focuses on leveraging existing technologies and practices to create a robust and efficient system.

## Context and Problem Statement

island.is will be maintaining and publishing content from many different government agencies and institutions. Their technical skill may vary a great deal, the content skill may also be lacking, therefore it is paramount for the system to be user friendly and intuitive.

Agencies and institutions should have enough autonomy with regards to editing content they are responsible for, to minimise the manual labour required by the island.is editors.

Which CMS system would best suit the needs of island.is?

## Decision Drivers

- Content needs to be editable by non technical users

- Content needs to be accessible across multiple domains and platforms

- Setup should be simple for developers new to the project

- The system should manage flexible content structures to limit systems impact on design

- The system should be user friendly and easy to use for a non technical person

- The system needs to offer a suitable workflow option to ease content management once multiple agencies start to contribute

## Considered Options

- [Gather content](https://gathercontent.com/)

- [Prismic](https://prismic.io/)

- [Contentful](https://contentful.com/)

- [Content stack](https://www.contentstack.com/)

- [Whitehall](https://docs.publishing.service.gov.uk/apps/whitehall.html)

- [Drupal](https://www.drupal.org/), [WordPress](https://wordpress.org/), [Strapi](https://strapi.io/), other hosted solutions,
human_decision:
# CMS

- Status: accepted
- Deciders: devs, management
- Date: 2020-05-25

## Decision Outcome

Devs narrowed the choice down to two options Contentful and Contentstack.

Both systems meet the required featureset.

A decision from management was made to use Contentful.  
Contentful is deemed to have a larger presence in the Icelandic dev community.  
Contentful is also believed to have a stronger funding base.  
Contentful is already implemented in some of our projects.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### [Gather content](https://gathercontent.com/)

- Good, because it allows for a great deal of workflow and collaboration features

- Good, because it can function as a project management tool for the content

- Good, because it can serve as a communication portal between island.is and the various parties involved

- Good, because it has comprehensive access control [7],[8]

- Bad, because it has a very basic API

- Bad, because it has a limited set of CMS features

- Bad, because it is not designed to be a publishing platform

### [Prismic](https://prismic.io/)

- Good, because it is kind of a simpler version of Contentful

- Good, because it is simple to use for non technical users

- Good, because image editing & placement is simple but effective

- Good, because it has a built-in CDN for images

- Bad, because it doesn't have a write API

- Bad, because the only way to import content is through the web interface

- Bad, because it doesn't have 'required fields'

- Bad, because API is somewhat limited

### [Contentful](https://contentful.com/)

- Good, because it has a best in class API and SDKs

- Good, because it has a large ecosystem of users

- Good, because it has a hybrid WYSIWYG editor, where you mix content types together with regular rich text content

- Good, because it is generic enough to be used for many different things

- Good, because it has a write-API, so you can create content programmatically

- Good, because it can be extended with UI extensions and various 3rd party tools

- Good, because it has rich access controls

- Good, because it a lot of developers have experience with it

- Good, because it has a built-in CDN for images and content

- Good, because it can define workflows and apply user permissions across those

- Bad, because it has a very generic structure and can be confusing for new users

- Bad, because depending on how content is set up, you may often have to do deep drill-downs on content entries

- Bad, because it has a complex billing structure making it harder to project costs

### [Contentstack](https://www.contentstack.com/)

- Good, because it is somewhat structured compared to some of the other choices

- Good, because it has an SDK for many languages

- Good, because it is generic enough to be used for many different things

- Good, because it has a write-API, so you can create content programmatically

- Good, because it can be extended with widgets and various 3rd party tools

- Good, because it has a built-in CDN for images and content

- Good, because it has rich access controls

- Good, because it can define workflows and apply user permissions across those

- Good, because it has a easy to understand billing structure

- Bad, because fewer developers have experience with it

- Bad, because it has a steep initial price

### [Drupal](https://www.drupal.org/), [WordPress](https://wordpress.org/) and other hosted solutions

- Good, because you have endless control over these types of systems

- Good, because there's a huge ecosystem of pre-built stuff

- Bad, because the huge ecosystem has a huge quality control problem

- Bad, because you will need specialised knowledge of these systems to maintain them and get out of them what you want.

- Bad, because you will have to host them yourself, take care of backups and maintenance and there are regular serious security breaches with these systems.

- Bad, because even though they can be run in headless mode, their UIs and APIs are not really geared towards headless usage.

### [Strapi](https://strapi.io/)

- Good, because you have endless control over these types of systems

- Good, because there's a huge ecosystem of pre-built stuff

- Good, because it has a write-API, so you can create content programmatically

- Bad, because you will need specialised knowledge of these systems to maintain them and get out of them what you want.

- Bad, because you will have to host them yourself, take care of backups and maintenance and there are regular serious security breaches with these systems.

- Bad, because the system is missing features deemed critical such as content internationalization and granular access control

### [Whitehall](https://docs.publishing.service.gov.uk/apps/whitehall.html)

- Good, because it manages workflows

- Bad, because it's built around Gov UK and we would have to fork the project to use it safely

- Bad, a lot of upfront work would be required to adapt the system to our stack

- Bad, because it does not fulfill the project's requirements

[gather content ACL tab 2](https://share.getcloudapp.com/04uP5684?embed=true)

Considering the decision drivers, **Contentful** and **WordPress** seem to be the most suitable options. Contentful offers flexibility and scalability, which is beneficial for managing content across multiple platforms. ## Context and Problem Statement

We are planning a refactor of tuf where:

* We do not want to try and support end-of-life versions of the language.
* We want to use modern language features, such as typing.
* We want to ease maintainer burden, by reducing the major language versions supported.

## Decision Drivers

* Python 2.7 is end-of-life
* Python 3.5 is end-of-life
* Modern Python allows use of desirable features such as type hints
* Supporting end-of-life Python versions adds maintenance overhead

## Considered Options

* Support Python 2.7 and 3.5+
* Support Python 2.7 and 3.6+
* Support Python 2.7 and 3.6+ (with polyfill modules)
* Support only Python 3.6+
human_decision:
# Default to Python 3.6 or newer for new development

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-10-20

Technical Story: https://github.com/theupdateframework/python-tuf/issues/1125

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Support only Python 3.6+", because we want modern features and lower
maintainer effort as we work to improve our codebase through the refactor effort.

New modules should target Python 3.6+.

Using modules to polyfill standard library features from Python 3.6+ feels
untenable as more libraries are dropping support for EOL Python releases.

### Negative Consequences

* Leaves major adopter and contributor without an actively developed client for some of
  their customers stuck on older Python versions.

## Links

* [Discussion of how/where to develop the refactored codebase](https://github.com/theupdateframework/python-tuf/issues/1126)
* [Discussion of deprecation policy for the pre-1.0, Python 2.7 supporting, code](https://github.com/theupdateframework/python-tuf/issues/1127)
debate_answer: Support only Python 3.6+
reason: The affirmative side aligns more closely with the decision drivers outlined in the context and problem statement. The primary goals are to avoid supporting end-of-life versions, leverage modern language features, and reduce maintenance burden. Supporting only Python 3.6+ directly addresses these goals by eliminating the need to maintain outdated versions, fully utilizing modern Python features, and aligning with the broader Python community's direction. While the negative side presents valid points about legacy systems and gradual transition, the overarching objectives of the refactor prioritize modernization and reduced maintenance overhead.
message_history:
Given the context and problem statement, the best option is to support only Python 3.6+. Here's why:

1. **End-of-Life Versions**: Both Python 2.7 and 3.5 are end-of-life. Continuing to support these versions would contradict the goal of not supporting end-of-life versions. Supporting only Python 3.6+ aligns with the decision driver to avoid end-of-life versions.

2. **Modern Language Features**: Python 3.6 introduced several modern features, including f-strings, variable annotations, and improvements to type hints. By supporting only Python 3.6+, you can fully leverage these features, which is in line with the goal of using modern language features.

3. **Maintenance Burden**: Supporting multiple major versions, especially those that are end-of-life, increases the maintenance burden. By focusing on Python 3.6+, you reduce the complexity and overhead associated with maintaining compatibility across multiple, outdated versions.

4. **Community and Ecosystem**: The Python community has largely moved on from Python 2.7 and 3.5. Many libraries and tools have dropped support for these versions, so aligning with Python 3.6+ ensures better compatibility and support from the broader ecosystem.

5. **Future-Proofing**: By choosing Python 3.6+ as the baseline, you position the project to more easily adopt future Python versions and features, ensuring long-term sustainability and relevance.

In conclusion, supporting only Python 3.6+ is the most logical choice given the decision drivers and the goals of the refactor. It minimizes maintenance overhead, allows the use of modern features, and aligns with the broader Python community's direction.
While supporting only Python 3.6+ seems like a strong option, there are reasons to consider supporting Python 2.7 and 3.6+ (with polyfill modules) as a viable alternative:

1. **Legacy Systems and Users**: Despite Python 2.7 being end-of-life, there are still legacy systems and users who rely on it. By supporting Python 2.7 with polyfill modules, you can cater to these users while still taking advantage of modern features in Python 3.6+.

2. **Gradual Transition**: Supporting both Python 2.7 and 3.6+ allows for a more gradual transition for users who are still on Python 2.7. This can be particularly important for organizations with large codebases that cannot be easily migrated to Python 3.6+.

3. **Polyfill Modules**: Using polyfill modules can help bridge the gap between Python 2.7 and 3.6+, allowing you to use some modern features while maintaining compatibility with older versions. This can reduce the immediate impact on users who have not yet upgraded.

4. **Broader User Base**: By supporting both versions, you maintain a broader user base, which can be beneficial for community support, contributions, and adoption of the project.

5. **Strategic Decision**: Depending on the project's goals and user demographics, maintaining support for Python 2.7 might be strategically advantageous, especially if a significant portion of the user base is still on this version.

In conclusion, while supporting only Python 3.6+ is a forward-looking choice, supporting Python 2.7 and 3.6+ with polyfill modules can provide a balanced approach that caters to both legacy users and those ready to adopt modern features. This option allows for a smoother transition and potentially wider adoption of the refactored project.

## Context and Problem Statement

We no longer want to depend on SQLCipher and want to use SQLite directly for build complexity and concerns over the long term future of the rust bindings. The encryption approach taken by SQLCipher means that in practice, the entire database is decrypted at startup, even if the logins functionality is not interacted with, defeating some of the benefits of using an encrypted database.

The per-field encryption in autofill, which we are planning to replicate in logins, separates the storage and encryption logic by limiting the storage layer to the management of encrypted data. Applying this approach in logins will break the existing validation and deduping code so we need a way to implement per-field encryption while supporting the validation and de-duping behavior.

## Decision Drivers

* Addressing previously identified deficiencies in the logins API while we are breaking the API for the encryption work
* Continuing to support the existing logins validation and deduping logic
* Avoiding the implementation of new security approaches that may require additional time and security resources
* Establishing a standard encyrption approach across components


## Considered Options

* Option 1 - Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions
* Option 2 - Keep the general shape of the API that is in place now - the app can pass the encryption key at any time to "unlock" the API, and re-lock it at any time, but the API in its entirety is only available when unlocked
