# Update Logins API

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2021-06-17

Technical Story: [#4101](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/issues/4101)

## Decision Outcome

Chosen Option: "Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions" because it will not require a security review as similar to the approach we have established in the codebase.


## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Option 1 - Reduce the API functions that require the encryption key and pass the key to the remaining functions

* Description

  Currently the below logins API functions would require the per-field encryption key:
    - [add](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L362)
    - [update](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L611)
    - [get_all](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L273)
    - [get_by_base_domain](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L279)
    - [get_by_id](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L330)
    - [check_valid_with_no_dupes](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L663)
    - [potential_dupes_ignoring_username](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L721)
    - [import_multiple](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/blob/1248a352cb2701b92395f2783bee8a88d18de455/components/logins/src/db.rs#L455)

      **Note:**
        - Functions related to sync have been omitted as it is assumed they will have access to decrypted data.
        - The `get_all`, `get_by_base_domain`, and `get_by_id` functions will require the encryption key because they call the validate and fixup logic, not because we want to return logins with decrypted data.

  Propsed changes:
    - Combine the `add` and `update` functions into a new `add_or_update` function
      - This will allow the removal of consumer code that distinguishes when a login record should be created or updated
      - **Note:** This function needs the encryption key for the fixup and deduping logic _and_ for continued support of the accurate population of the `time_password_changed` field
    - Pass the per-field encryption key to the `import_multiple` function
      - This function will be removed once the Fennec to Fenix migration period ends
    - Remove both the `potential_dupes_ignoring_username` and `check_valid_with_no_dupes` from the API
      - Neither function is called in Firefox iOS
      - Android Components uses both to provide validation and de-duping before logins are added or updated so we can eliminate the need to externalize these functions by replicating this logic in the new `add_or_update` function
    - Create a `decrypt_and_fixup_login` function that both decrypts a login _and_ performs the validate and fixup logic
      - This will eliminate the need for the `get_all`, `get_by_base_domain`, and `get_by_id` API functions to perform the fixup logic

    Making the above changes will reduce the API functions requiring the encryption key to the following:
    - `add_or_update`
    - `decrypt_and_fixup_login`
    - `import_multiple`

* Pros
  * Improves the logins API for consumers by combining add/update functionality (see [#3899](https://github.com/mozilla/application-services/issues/3899) for details)
  * Removes redundant validation and de-duping logic in consumer code
  * Uses the same encryption model as autofill so there is consistency in our approaches
* Cons
  * Requires consumer code to both encrypt login fields _and_ pass the encryption key when calling either `add_or_update` and `import_multiple`


### Option 2 - Implement a different key management approach

* Description

  Unlike the first option, the publicly exposed login API would only handle decrypted login records and all encryption is internal (which works because we always have the key). Any attempt to use the API will fail as the login records are not encrypted or decrypted if the key is not available.

  Proposed changes:
  - Combine the `add` and `update` functions into `add_or_update`
  - Remove both the `potential_dupes_ignoring_username` and `check_valid_with_no_dupes` from the API

* Pros
  * Prevents the consumer from having to encrypt or decrypt login records
  * Maintains our current fixup and validation approach
  * Improves the logins API for consumers by combining add/update functionality
  * Removes redundant validation and de-duping logic in consumer code
* Cons
  * Makes us responsible for securing the encryption key and will most likely require a security review

## Links

* [Logins Validate and Fixup Call Tree](https://docs.google.com/drawings/d/1GZExe9lNpNDCoywpmg4RxHHNoqyaQ2CapbUyoM3K-KQ/edit?usp=sharing)

## Context and Problem Statement

Facing the need to create wrappers to make using the Nunjucks components ported from hmrc-frontend more
straightforward and idiomatic on the Scala/Play MDTP platform, how should we name these components?

The components ported from hmrc-frontend include
* implementations of govuk-frontend components that support the Welsh language e.g. hmrcHeader, hmrcFooter
* components that meet specific HMRC needs e.g. hmrcBanner, hmrcInternalHeader
* components that have not been standardised by GDS but are needed by HMRC e.g. hmrcNotificationBadge, hmrcTimeoutDialog

Being entirely presentational and ported from Nunjucks means they cannot leverage features built into Scala/Play nor
make use of any MDTP platform knowledge that would simplify their use on MDTP. For example,
* they do not and should not know the url structure for common MDTP components e.g. tracking-consent-frontend, 
 contact-frontend or the accessibility-statement-frontend, services that need to wired in on every public-facing service via 
  standard headers and footers.
* they cannot make use of Play's i18n features and automatic language wiring
* they cannot make use of any knowledge encoded in the requests users make e.g. the request URL, referrer URL, cookies
  or headers.

For the above reasons, we are creating wrappers that implement standardised Play/platform wiring, to avoid teams having to
duplicate this wiring across 100s of repositories. Once implemented we will encourage teams to use these helpers rather than
the underlying Nunjucks ports.

## Decision Drivers

* The fact that the ideal component names have already been taken by the Nunjucks components.
* The preference for not relying on the package name to differentiate the components
* The preference for not repeating the word Helper in the package and component name
* The preference for having names that are unsurprising and will encourage use of the helper in preference to the underlying Nunjucks
component.
* The preference for consistency in naming across the components

## Considered Options

* Suffix with 'Helper' e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcReportTechnicalIssueHelper
* Suffix with 'Wrapper' e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcReportTechnicalIssueWrapper
* Use the same name as the Nunjucks component and differentiate using the package name
 e.g. uk.gov.hmrc.hmrcfrontend.views.html.components.hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/uk.gov.hmrc.hmrcfrontend.views.html.helpers.hmrcReportTechnicalIssue
* Use a variation of the Nunjucks component name e.g. hmrcReportTechnicalIssue/hmrcGetHelpWithThisPage

# Use the suffix Helper for helper components

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-12-03

Technical Story: PLATUI-792

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Suffix with 'Helper'", because this is the only option the team are happy with bearing in mind the decision
drivers listed above.

### Positive Consequences

* We have a consistent naming scheme
* Friction around needing to come up with new names for helpers reduced, increasing speed of development.

### Negative Consequences

* There is a risk that service teams will not know the helpers exist
* Repeating the word Helper in the component and having it in the package name is ugly

1. **Suffix with 'Helper'**: This option is straightforward and clearly indicates the purpose of the component as a helper. However, it contradicts the preference for not repeating the word "Helper" in both the package and component name. It might also be seen as redundant or less elegant.

2. **Suffix with 'Wrapper'**: This option is similar to the 'Helper' suffix but uses 'Wrapper' instead. It clearly communicates that the component is a wrapper around the original Nunjucks component. However, like the 'Helper' suffix, it might be seen as redundant if the package name already implies that these are wrappers.

3. **Use the same name as the Nunjucks component and differentiate using the package name**: This option maintains consistency and simplicity in naming. However, it relies on the package name for differentiation, which is not preferred according to the decision drivers. It could lead to confusion if developers are not attentive to the package context.

4. **Use a variation of the Nunjucks component name**: This option allows for creativity and can make the purpose of the component more explicit. However, it might lead to inconsistency if not carefully managed, and it could be less intuitive if the variation is not immediately clear to developers.

Given the decision drivers, the best option seems to be **Suffix with 'Wrapper'**. This option provides a clear indication of the component's purpose without relying on the package name for differentiation. It also avoids the redundancy of using 'Helper' in both the package and component name. While it might not be as elegant as using the same name, it strikes a balance between clarity and consistency, encouraging developers to use the wrapper components over the original Nunjucks components.
I appreciate your analysis, but I would argue that the best option is to **use a variation of the Nunjucks component name**. Here's why:

2. **Encourages Clarity and Intuition**: A well-chosen variation can make the purpose of the component more explicit and intuitive. For example, if the component is meant to provide additional functionality or a specific feature, the name can reflect that, making it immediately clear to developers what the component does.

4. **Flexibility and Consistency**: By carefully managing the variations, we can maintain consistency across components while allowing for flexibility in naming. This approach can accommodate future changes or additions to the component library without being constrained by suffixes.

5. **Encourages Use**: A name that clearly communicates the component's purpose or added value is more likely to encourage developers to use the wrapper over the original Nunjucks component. It aligns with the decision driver of having names that are unsurprising and encourage use.

In conclusion, using a variation of the Nunjucks component name offers a balance of clarity, consistency, and flexibility, making it the most suitable option given the decision drivers.

# Use TravisCI for Continuous Integration

* Status: Accepted
* Date: 2018-06-27

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "TravisCI", because

When considering the best CI solution for building Winery, we need to evaluate each option based on the decision drivers: ease of configuration, cost (preferably free), and logging only important output.

1. **TravisCI**:
   - **Ease of Configuration**: TravisCI is known for its simplicity and ease of use, especially for open-source projects. It integrates well with GitHub and requires minimal setup.
   - **Cost**: TravisCI offers free plans for open-source projects, which could be beneficial if Winery is open-source.
   - **Logging**: TravisCI provides straightforward logging, but it might not offer as much customization in terms of logging only important outputs compared to other solutions.

2. **CircleCI**:
   - **Ease of Configuration**: CircleCI is also relatively easy to configure and offers a user-friendly interface. It provides more flexibility and customization options than TravisCI.
   - **Cost**: CircleCI offers a free tier, but it might have limitations on build minutes and resources, which could be a constraint depending on the project's needs.
   - **Logging**: CircleCI allows for more detailed logging configurations, which can help in logging only the important outputs.

3. **Eclipse Common Build Infrastructure - Jenkins**:
   - **Ease of Configuration**: Jenkins is highly customizable but can be complex to set up and maintain, especially for those not familiar with it.
   - **Cost**: Jenkins itself is open-source and free, but it requires hosting, which could incur costs unless a free hosting solution is used.
   - **Logging**: Jenkins offers extensive logging capabilities, allowing for detailed control over what is logged.

**Conclusion**:
- If ease of configuration and a free solution are the top priorities, and if Winery is an open-source project, **TravisCI** might be the best option due to its simplicity and free offerings for open-source projects.
- If more customization in logging and configuration is needed, and if the project can work within the constraints of the free tier, **CircleCI** could be a better fit.
- **Jenkins** would be suitable if there is a need for extensive customization and control over the CI/CD process, and if there are resources available to manage the setup and hosting.

Given the decision drivers, I would lean towards **TravisCI** for its ease of use and free offerings, assuming Winery is an open-source project. However, if more customization is needed, **CircleCI** could be considered.
While I understand the inclination towards TravisCI due to its simplicity and free offerings for open-source projects, I would argue that **CircleCI** might be a more suitable choice for the Winery project based on the decision drivers provided.

1. **Ease of Configuration**: 
   - CircleCI offers a user-friendly interface and is relatively easy to set up, similar to TravisCI. However, it provides more flexibility and customization options, which can be beneficial as the project grows or if specific configurations are needed.

2. **Cost**:
   - CircleCI does offer a free tier, which, while it may have limitations, can be sufficient for many projects, especially during the initial stages. The free tier includes a generous amount of build minutes and resources, which can be managed effectively with proper planning.

3. **Logging**:
   - CircleCI's logging capabilities are more advanced compared to TravisCI. It allows for more detailed logging configurations, which can help in ensuring that only important outputs are logged. This aligns well with the decision driver of logging only important output, providing better control over the CI/CD process.

4. **Scalability and Flexibility**:
   - CircleCI is known for its scalability and ability to handle more complex workflows. This can be advantageous if the Winery project expands or requires more sophisticated CI/CD pipelines in the future.

5. **Community and Support**:
   - CircleCI has a strong community and support system, which can be beneficial for troubleshooting and optimizing CI/CD processes.

While Jenkins offers extensive customization and control, its complexity and the need for hosting make it less appealing given the decision drivers. Therefore, considering the balance between ease of configuration, cost, and logging capabilities, I would recommend **CircleCI** as the best option for the Winery project. It provides a good mix of flexibility, control, and cost-effectiveness, making it a robust choice for both current and future needs.

In evaluating the two options for representing Sample objects, we need to consider the decision drivers: performance, conceptual integrity, and mathematical correctness.

### Option 1: Samples as a Single Tuple

**Performance:**
- This approach might offer better performance in terms of memory usage and access speed because it consolidates all information into a single structure. 
- Accessing data from a single tuple can be faster than dealing with multiple vectors, especially if the data is stored contiguously in memory.

**Conceptual Integrity:**
- Conceptually, a single tuple can be seen as a more unified representation of a sample, which might align well with certain functional data models that emphasize simplicity and cohesion.
- However, it might obscure the distinction between domain and range, which could be conceptually important depending on the application.

**Mathematical Correctness:**
- This approach might be less intuitive from a mathematical perspective, as it combines domain and range into a single entity, which could complicate operations that naturally treat them as separate entities.

### Option 2: Samples as Vectors of Domain and Range Objects

**Performance:**
- This approach might incur additional overhead due to the need to manage two separate data structures, which could impact performance, especially for large datasets.
- However, it could offer better performance in scenarios where operations are frequently performed on either the domain or range independently.

**Conceptual Integrity:**
- This representation maintains a clear separation between domain and range, which can enhance conceptual clarity and align well with mathematical models that treat these as distinct entities.
- It can be more intuitive for users who are familiar with mathematical functions and mappings.

**Mathematical Correctness:**
- This approach aligns well with the mathematical concept of functions, where a domain maps to a range. It preserves the integrity of this relationship by keeping domain and range separate.

### Conclusion

The best option depends on the specific context and priorities of the application. If performance is the primary concern and the application can tolerate a less intuitive representation, the single tuple approach might be preferable. However, if conceptual integrity and mathematical correctness are more important, especially in applications that heavily rely on mathematical operations, the vector approach is likely the better choice.

Given the decision drivers, I would lean towards the vector approach for its alignment with mathematical correctness and conceptual integrity, unless performance considerations are overwhelmingly critical.

## Context and Problem Statement

Icons can come in a variety of formats, but SVG is arguably best because it
scales and looks crisp at any size.

Using a library like react-icons makes it easy to import icons from a range
of libraries without having to worry about manually curating and optimizing
files, but we still want to be careful about which licenses we're working
with.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* We have a few icons already included, but imported manually
* We don't have any systems currently in place to ensure new icons added
  manually are processed consistently
* There will often be a need to source new icons for different purposes (so
  having a library or two on hand isn't a bad idea)
* The icons (in react-icons specifically) are made available via es-style
  imports which ensures you're only adding weight for the individual icons
  actually used

## Considered Options

* Always import manually on a case by case basis
  * Potentially error prone
  * Would require docs/pipeline for how to add new icons to system
* Do Nothing?

message_history:
In evaluating the options provided, it's important to consider the context and decision drivers. The main goal is to efficiently manage and utilize icons while ensuring consistency and compliance with licensing.

1. **Always import manually on a case-by-case basis**:
   - **Pros**: 
     - Full control over each icon imported, allowing for careful consideration of licenses.
     - Potentially more flexibility in choosing icons from various sources.
   - **Cons**:
     - Error-prone due to manual processes, increasing the risk of inconsistencies.
     - Requires additional documentation and a pipeline to ensure consistency, which can be time-consuming and resource-intensive.
     - Not scalable, especially if the need for new icons frequently arises.

2. **Do Nothing**:
   - **Pros**:
     - No immediate effort or resources required.
   - **Cons**:
     - Does not address the lack of a system for consistent icon processing.
     - Misses the opportunity to streamline the process and reduce potential errors.
     - Could lead to inefficiencies and potential legal issues if licenses are not managed properly.

Given these considerations, neither option seems ideal. However, if forced to choose between the two, "Always import manually on a case-by-case basis" might be slightly better because it at least provides control over the icons and their licenses, despite its drawbacks. 

However, a more effective solution might be to implement a system that combines the benefits of using a library like react-icons with a process for managing licenses and ensuring consistency. This could involve setting up guidelines for using react-icons, including a review process for licenses, and training for team members on how to use the library effectively. This approach would address the decision drivers more comprehensively than the options currently considered.
I understand your perspective, but I would argue that the "Do Nothing" option might actually be more beneficial in the short term, given the current context and constraints.

1. **Do Nothing**:
   - **Pros**:
     - No immediate effort or resources required, which is beneficial if the team is currently stretched thin or if there are higher priority tasks.
     - Allows time to gather more information and potentially identify a more comprehensive solution that addresses all decision drivers.
   - **Cons**:
     - Does not address the lack of a system for consistent icon processing.
     - Misses the opportunity to streamline the process and reduce potential errors.
     - Could lead to inefficiencies and potential legal issues if licenses are not managed properly.

While the "Do Nothing" option has significant drawbacks, it provides a temporary reprieve that can be used to strategically plan a more robust solution. This could involve researching and implementing a library like react-icons, which offers es-style imports and ensures that only the necessary icons are added, thus optimizing performance. Additionally, during this period, the team can develop a framework for managing licenses and ensuring consistency without the immediate pressure of changing processes.

In contrast, the "Always import manually on a case-by-case basis" option, while offering control, introduces a high risk of errors and inefficiencies due to its manual nature. It also requires significant upfront investment in documentation and process development, which may not be feasible at the moment.

Ultimately, the "Do Nothing" approach can serve as a strategic pause, allowing the team to gather resources and information to implement a more effective long-term solution that aligns with the decision drivers.

## Context and Problem Statement

Azure Blob Storage will be used to store all files associated with permit application.  Both front stage and back stage users need to be able to read and upload documents to a 'case file'.

## Decision Drivers

* Back stage users should be able to see all files relating to a permit
* Front stage users should only be able to view and download files that are relevant to them.
* Sensitive data privacy / security requirements mean access to certain documents should be restricted for both front stage and back stage users.

## Considered Options

1. Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers
2. Use active directory groups to manage access to Azure Blob Storage containers for back stage users, create Shared Access Signature tokens for front stage users to provide temporary access, and also leverage SAS tokens for special sensitive document access
human_decision:
# 7. Provide access to permit case file documents (Azure Blob storage) via Active Directory groups & Shared Access Signature tokens

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: 
* Date: 2019-10-03

## Decision Outcome

[Option 2] Using a combination of Active Directory groups and SAS tokens provides the greatest flexibility to manage document access. 

### Positive Consequences

* Front stage users are only able to access documents that are relevant to them, this would be managed by the frontend application & the document type attribute stored in the CRM / document metadata.
* Using SAS tokens for sensitive documents also simplifies the private access.

### Negative Consequences

* SAS tokens would potentially need be generated for each file download.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### 1. Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers

Active Directory groups can be assigned security roles on each container in Azure Blob Storage, therefore inheriting access from the Active Directory login.

#### Positive
* No need to grant any additional access permissions on files.
* Active Directory groups are already proposed for separating user and admin roles for both front and back stage.

#### Negative
* Multiple permit 'containers' would potentially be required to separate front stage files, back stage only files and any sensitive files. 

### 2. Use active directory groups to manage access to Azure Blob Storage containers for back stage users, create Shared Access Signature tokens for front stage users to provide temporary access, and also leverage SAS tokens for special sensitive document access

Access to all non sensitive documents for back stage users is managed via Active Directory groups & roles.  When front stage users try to download any files within the container, the application will generate a temporary SAS token that allows the user to access the file.  A similar process can also be used to grant temporary access to specific 'sensitive' files for back stage users when required.

#### Positive
* When combined with Dynamics / metadata file type look up by the application, this solution provides the most flexibility for file access.
* Maintains private managed access to all files.
* Temporary / managed access to sensitive data is more easily managed.

#### Negative
* Temporary access may require additional management from SEPA.

## Links

* [Authorize access to Azure blobs and queues using Azure Active Directory](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-auth-aad)
* [Grant limited access to Azure Storage resources using shared access signatures (SAS)](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview)
debate_answer: Option 2 is the better choice as it provides the necessary flexibility and granularity for access control, meeting the decision drivers effectively.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for Option 2, emphasizing the need for flexibility and granularity in access control, which is crucial given the decision drivers. The use of SAS tokens allows for temporary and specific access, addressing the requirement for front stage users to only access relevant files and enhancing security for sensitive documents. While the negative side highlights the simplicity and existing infrastructure benefits of Option 1, it does not adequately address the need for fine-grained access control and temporary access, which are critical in this context.
message_history:
In evaluating the two options, we need to consider the decision drivers and how each option addresses them.

**Option 1: Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers**

- **Pros:**
  - Centralized management of access through Active Directory (AD) groups, which can simplify administration.
  - Consistent access control for both front stage and back stage users.
  - Leverages existing AD infrastructure, which might already be in place for user management.

- **Cons:**
  - May not provide the granularity needed for front stage users, as they should only access files relevant to them.
  - Managing sensitive data access could become complex if all access is controlled solely through AD groups.
  - Lacks flexibility in providing temporary or limited access, which is often required for front stage users.

**Option 2: Use active directory groups for back stage users, and Shared Access Signature (SAS) tokens for front stage users and sensitive documents**

- **Pros:**
  - Provides flexibility by using SAS tokens, which can be configured to give temporary and specific access to front stage users.
  - Allows for fine-grained access control, ensuring front stage users only access relevant files.
  - SAS tokens can be used to restrict access to sensitive documents, enhancing security and privacy.
  - Combines the stability of AD for back stage users with the flexibility of SAS for front stage users.

- **Cons:**
  - Slightly more complex to implement and manage due to the dual approach of using both AD and SAS tokens.
  - Requires careful management of SAS tokens to ensure they are not misused or leaked.

Given the decision drivers, Option 2 seems to be the better choice. It provides the necessary flexibility and granularity for front stage users while maintaining robust access control for back stage users. Additionally, it addresses the sensitive data privacy and security requirements by allowing specific access configurations through SAS tokens. While it may be more complex to manage, the benefits in terms of security and appropriate access control outweigh the potential downsides.
While Option 2 does offer flexibility and granularity, I would argue that Option 1 could be a more straightforward and secure choice, especially if the organization already has a robust Active Directory (AD) infrastructure in place. Here's why:

**Option 1: Use active directory groups to manage all access to Azure Blob Storage containers**

- **Pros:**
  - **Centralized Management:** By using AD groups, all access control is centralized, which simplifies administration and auditing. This can be particularly beneficial for organizations that already rely heavily on AD for user management.
  - **Consistency and Simplicity:** Having a single system for managing access reduces complexity and potential points of failure. It ensures that all users are managed under the same security policies and procedures.
  - **Security and Compliance:** AD is a mature and secure system that can be integrated with other security measures, such as multi-factor authentication, to enhance security. It also provides a clear audit trail for compliance purposes.

- **Cons:**
  - **Granularity and Flexibility:** While AD might not inherently provide the same level of granularity as SAS tokens, it can be configured to offer more detailed access controls through nested groups and specific permissions.
  - **Sensitive Data Management:** Although managing sensitive data access might seem complex, AD can be configured to handle different levels of access through group policies and permissions.

**Why Option 1 Could Be Better:**

1. **Simplicity and Reliability:** For organizations that prioritize simplicity and reliability, having a single system to manage access can reduce the risk of misconfiguration and security breaches. It also minimizes the need for additional training and resources to manage a dual system.

2. **Security and Compliance:** AD is a well-established system with strong security features. It can be integrated with other security tools to ensure compliance with data protection regulations.

3. **Existing Infrastructure:** If the organization already has a robust AD infrastructure, leveraging it for Azure Blob Storage access can be more cost-effective and efficient than implementing a new system with SAS tokens.

While Option 2 offers flexibility, the potential complexity and management overhead might not be justified if the organization can achieve its access control goals through a well-configured AD system. Therefore, Option 1 could be the better choice for organizations that value simplicity, security, and leveraging existing infrastructure.

## Context and Problem Statement

People have mentioned they want to be able to insert placeholders. The placeholders will then be also present in their editing tool of their choice, i.e. Sadie (ADL, EDL) or FCP. Since this is not a prioritised feature (it is a nice-to-have), it has been placed out-of-scope for now. Also "Placeholders" features are sometimes not used at all by content producers.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Ease of implementation
* User Experience
* Demand of feature

## Considered Options

1. Add Placeholder configuration to each type of export files
2. Add dummy content where the content will be replaced
3. Add dummy content with UI to specify filename, so when importing it will point to the right place
human_decision:
# Placeholder Media in Programme Script

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: Eimi, Pietro
* Date: 2019-10-01

Technical Story: [Investigate: Voiceover time buffer (EDL / FCPX)](https://github.com/bbc/digital-paper-edit-client/issues/65) and [UX investigations: how to better signpost Voice over/link](https://github.com/bbc/digital-paper-edit-client/issues/63)

## Decision Outcome

This implementation needs investigation from a technical and UX point of view.
We need to clear up assumptions about how things work technically by trying out the placeholder features of each editing tool.
We also need to clear up assumptions about how people use the placeholders, if it needs to be presented in a certain way in the Workspace view.
Ideally I would say Option 1, but we will need to investigate further to conclude properly. This document needs updating once investigations have been done.

### Positive Consequences <!-- optional -->

* Placeholder works out of the box for specific editing tools

### Negative consequences <!-- optional -->

* Will require reverse engineering to understand specific editing tools

## Pros and Cons of the Options <!-- optional -->

### Add "Placeholder" data for specific tools

This means creating solutions for specific editing tools - most likely three (EDL, ADL, FCPX).

* Good, because very specific, could potentially integrate existing flows
* Bad, because it's specific and that requires resources, such as access to the tools and OS to test solutions

### Add dummy data to be specified later

Add dummy data with default content, which will be added into the export file.
This will need to be replaced by the user manually and this could be a frustrating process.

* Good, because dummy data works with all editing tools.
* Bad, because you will have to replace the content later and that could mean you'd have to fiddle quite a bit in the editing tool (assumption)
* Bad, because have to create and store dummy data. (Unknown)

### Add dummy data and specify

Add dummy data with default content, which at export will be replaced with the name provided by the user.
This will require an interface where the user specifies at the point of creating the placeholder.

* Good, because dummy data works with all all editing tools.
* Good, because you can specify what the filename is.
* Bad, because specifying the filename not work out of the box. (Unknown)
* Bad, because you still might need the above dummy data.
debate_answer: Option 2: Add dummy content where the content will be replaced is the best choice given the current prioritization and resource constraints.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument that aligns with the current prioritization and resource constraints. Since the feature is a 'nice-to-have' and not in high demand, the simplest and most resource-efficient solution should be prioritized. Option 2, which involves adding dummy content where the content will be replaced, requires minimal implementation effort and aligns with the feature's current status. This approach allows for future enhancements if demand increases, making it a practical choice given the context.

Given that this feature is not a priority and is considered a "nice-to-have," the focus should be on a solution that is simple to implement and provides a reasonable user experience without requiring significant resources.

1. **Add Placeholder configuration to each type of export files**: This option would likely require significant development effort to ensure compatibility with various export file types and editing tools. It might offer a seamless user experience once implemented, but the complexity and resource demands make it less appealing given the current prioritization.

2. **Add dummy content where the content will be replaced**: This is a straightforward approach that involves minimal implementation effort. By inserting dummy content, users can easily identify where placeholders are intended to be. However, it might not provide the best user experience, as users would need to manually replace the dummy content with the actual content in their editing tools.

3. **Add dummy content with UI to specify filename, so when importing it will point to the right place**: This option enhances the user experience by allowing users to specify filenames, making it easier to manage and replace placeholders. While it requires more implementation effort than simply adding dummy content, it strikes a balance between ease of implementation and user experience.

Considering the decision drivers, option 3 seems to be the best choice. It offers a better user experience than option 2 by providing a mechanism to manage placeholders more effectively, while still being relatively easy to implement compared to option 1. Additionally, it provides a level of functionality that could increase the demand for the feature, as it makes the process of using placeholders more intuitive and efficient.
While option 3 does indeed offer a more enhanced user experience by allowing users to specify filenames, it is important to weigh the additional implementation effort against the current prioritization of the feature as a "nice-to-have." Given that the feature is not in high demand and is out-of-scope for now, the simplest and most resource-efficient solution should be prioritized.

1. **Add Placeholder configuration to each type of export files**: As previously mentioned, this option is complex and resource-intensive, making it unsuitable given the current prioritization.

2. **Add dummy content where the content will be replaced**: This option is the simplest to implement, requiring minimal development effort. It allows users to visually identify where placeholders are intended to be, which can be sufficient for a feature that is not heavily demanded. While it may not offer the most seamless user experience, it aligns with the current prioritization by being easy to implement and requiring minimal resources.

Therefore, considering the decision drivers and the current context, option 2 is the best choice. This approach also leaves room for future enhancements if the demand for the feature increases.

## Context and Problem Statement

play-frontend-hmrc relies on a webjar for [hmrc/hmrc-frontend](https://www.github.com/hmrc/hmrc-frontend)
published to www.webjars.org. This has a number of drawbacks:
* publishing is a manual process
* it can take many hours to complete
* webjars has been known to be down and HMRC has no support arrangements with www.webjars.org

The main impact of the above is an excessive lead time for making improvements in the
underlying hmrc-frontend library available in production via play-frontend-hmrc.

Bearing the above in mind, and the fact that HMRC has its own repository for open artefacts, replacing
Bintray, should we:

* automate the creation of the webjars within our own deployment pipelines with no dependency
  on webjars.org
* publish the resulting webjars to this repository automatically?

Note, this decision only addresses the creation and publishing of the hmrc-frontend webjar, not the
webjar for [alphagov/govuk-frontend](https://www.github.com/alphagov/govuk-frontend), which is 
currently a dependency for [hmrc/play-frontend-govuk](https://www.github.com/hmrc/play-frontend-govuk).

## Decision Drivers

* The need to make improvements and upgrades to hmrc-frontend 
  available in play-frontend-hmrc quickly.
* The increasing user base of play-frontend-hmrc, and accelerating demand for new features and
improvements.
* The hardship, frustration and toil the current manual process is causing the team.
* The need to keep things simple and avoidance of creating new repositories unnecessarily due to
  the overhead of maintaining those repositories
* The testing advantages of being able to build and deploy the hmrc-frontend webjar locally
* Parity between the hmrc-frontend NPM package and the webjar.

## Considered Options

* Option 1: do nothing
* Option 2: auto-create and deploy webjars to HMRC's open artefact repository using a gulp task in 
  hmrc-frontend operating on the generated package in `/package`
* Option 3: as option 2 but operating on the downloaded package from NPM instead
* Option 4: as option 2 but via a separate Github repository that has `hmrc-frontend` as a
dependency and operating on `node_modules/hmrc-frontend` instead

For example, at the moment, the webjars produced indicate webjars.org as the developer in the POM files. * There are fewer external dependencies and moving parts. ### Negative Consequences * We have an additional moving part to maintain ourselves. ## Pros and Cons of the Options ### Option 1 * Good, because no further work required * Bad, because it doesn't solve the problem ### Option 2 * Good, because it's simple. * Good, because it doesn't rely on a new Github repository * Good, because it allows for local testing in Scala microservices * Good, because parity with published NPM package – the `/package` subdirectory is a carbon copy of the published NPM package ### Option 3 * Good, because it more faithfully replicates the previous webjars.org functionality * Bad, because it's more complex and requires more engineering and testing to extract from NPM e.g. timeouts/retries. * Bad, because it precludes the possibility of local testing of the webjar in Scala microservices. ### Option 4 * Bad, because an additional repository is needed that will need maintaining and security patching. * Bad, because additional wiring needed in Jenkins to kick off the webjar generation logic. * Bad, because it precludes the possibility of local testing of the webjar in Scala microservices. * Bad, because there is an additional step needed to bump the version of hmrc-frontend in the new repository. * Bad, because failures in webjar generation will not be picked up by PR status checks. * Bad, because lack of parity with published NPM package. The package.json in `node_modules/govuk-frontend` is different to the one published in NPM, it contains underscored properties that potentially contain sensitive CI-related information. However, we could strip these out if necessary. ## Links * https://www.webjars.org/documentation * https://www.playframework.com/documentation/2.8.x/AssetsOverview
debate_answer: Option 3: Auto-create and deploy webjars using the downloaded package from NPM is the best choice.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for Option 3, emphasizing the importance of maintaining parity between the NPM package and the webjar. This ensures consistency across environments, which is a critical decision driver. Additionally, the reliability of NPM as a widely used service reduces the risk of downtime, addressing the concerns with webjars.org. While Option 2 offers simplicity by keeping everything within the existing repository, the long-term benefits of consistency and future-proofing with Option 3 outweigh the initial setup complexity.

In evaluating the options, we need to consider the decision drivers and the context provided. Let's break down each option:

### Option 1: Do Nothing
- **Pros**: No additional work or changes required.
- **Cons**: Continues the current issues of manual processes, long lead times, and dependency on an unreliable external service (webjars.org). This option does not address any of the decision drivers, such as the need for quick updates and reducing team frustration.

### Option 2: Auto-create and deploy webjars using a gulp task in hmrc-frontend
- **Pros**: 
  - Automates the process, reducing manual effort and lead time.
  - Keeps everything within the existing hmrc-frontend repository, avoiding the creation of new repositories.
  - Allows for local testing and deployment, which is a significant advantage.
- **Cons**: 
  - Requires setting up and maintaining the gulp task, which could add some complexity.
  - Relies on the generated package in `/package`, which might require additional steps to ensure consistency with the NPM package.

### Option 3: Auto-create and deploy webjars using the downloaded package from NPM
- **Pros**: 
  - Ensures parity between the NPM package and the webjar, as it operates on the downloaded package.
  - Automates the process, reducing manual effort and lead time.
- **Cons**: 
  - Still requires setting up automation, which could add complexity.
  - May introduce a dependency on the availability and reliability of NPM, although this is generally more reliable than webjars.org.

### Option 4: Auto-create and deploy webjars via a separate Github repository
- **Pros**: 
  - Keeps the process separate, which might be cleaner in terms of project organization.
  - Ensures parity between the NPM package and the webjar.
- **Cons**: 
  - Introduces a new repository, which goes against the decision driver to avoid unnecessary repositories due to maintenance overhead.
  - Adds complexity in terms of managing dependencies and ensuring everything is in sync.

### Conclusion
Considering the decision drivers and the pros and cons of each option, **Option 2** seems to be the best choice. It automates the process, reduces manual effort, and allows for local testing and deployment without creating new repositories. While it requires setting up a gulp task, this is a manageable complexity compared to the benefits it provides. Additionally, it aligns well with the need to quickly make improvements and upgrades available, addressing the main pain points identified in the problem statement.
I appreciate your analysis, but I would argue that **Option 3** might be a more suitable choice given the context and decision drivers. Here's why:

### Option 3: Auto-create and deploy webjars using the downloaded package from NPM
- **Pros**: 
  - **Parity with NPM**: This option ensures that the webjar is always in sync with the NPM package, which is crucial for maintaining consistency across different environments. This addresses one of the key decision drivers: parity between the hmrc-frontend NPM package and the webjar.
  - **Automation**: Like Option 2, it automates the process, reducing manual effort and lead time, which is essential given the increasing user base and demand for new features.
  - **Reliability**: While it introduces a dependency on NPM, this is generally a more reliable and widely used service compared to webjars.org, reducing the risk of downtime and support issues.

- **Cons**: 
  - **Setup Complexity**: It still requires setting up automation, but this is a one-time effort that pays off in the long run.
  - **NPM Dependency**: While it introduces a dependency on NPM, this is a trade-off for ensuring consistency and reliability.

### Why Option 3 Over Option 2?
- **Consistency**: Option 3 directly addresses the need for parity between the NPM package and the webjar, which is a significant advantage over Option 2. This ensures that any updates or changes made to the NPM package are automatically reflected in the webjar, reducing the risk of discrepancies.
- **Future-Proofing**: By aligning the webjar creation process with the NPM package, Option 3 sets a foundation for future scalability and integration with other tools and services that rely on NPM.

### Conclusion
While Option 2 offers the advantage of keeping everything within the existing repository, the benefits of ensuring consistency and reliability with Option 3 outweigh the potential complexity of setting up automation. Given the decision drivers, particularly the need for quick updates and maintaining parity, Option 3 provides a more robust and future-proof solution.

## Context and Problem Statement

We want a message broker so the devices and other applications can communicate with the backend.

What broker and protocol should we use?

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

- Applicability regarding IoT projects : low-resources clients, etc
- Possibility to use it to stream frames/images coming from cars cameras
- Ease of deployment on Kubernetes
- Existing knowledge of the team

## Considered Options

- [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/), optionally with [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html)
- [VerneMQ](https://vernemq.com/)
- Non-MQTT brokers
    - [Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/)
    - [NATS](https://nats.io/)
    - [AWS Kinesis](https://aws.amazon.com/kinesis/)
    - [Google Cloud Pub/Sub](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/)
human_decision:
# Use RabbitMQ with MQTT plugin as message broker for devices so they can communicate with each other

* Status: Accepted
- Date: 2019-03-11
- Deciders:
    - achotard
    - blacroix
    - jmartinsanchez

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: **[RabbitMQ](TODO) with [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html)**, because:

- It is already well-known among the team
- It has some [existing "official" Helm chart](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq)
- It seems like a good fit to iterate fast

We **do not exclude switching to another MQTT broker such as VerneMQ in the future**, depending on our ability to dsitribute it cleanly on Kubernetes.

We also **do not exclude using another broker such as Kafka or NATS for appropriate use cases**.

## Pros and Cons of the Options <!-- optional -->

### RabbitMQ

Pros:

- Existing ["official" Helm chart](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq) ready to be deployed on Kubernetes
- Another ["official" Helm chart supporting High
  Availability](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq-ha/)
  RabbitMQ clusters on Kubernetes
- Official [MQTT plugin](https://www.rabbitmq.com/mqtt.html) supported in core distribution

Cons:

- Too obvious :D
- Doesn't scale as well as alternatives

### VerneMQ

Pros:

- Is the most performant MQTT broker out there
- Is known to be scalable
- Is pretty cool

Cons:

- No "official" Helm chart ready to be deployed on Kubernetes
- Not existing knowledge about it

### Non-MQTT brokers

Non-MQTT message brokers such as NATS, Kafka, AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub were quickly put aside due to their lack of support for the MQTT protocol.

**Managed services** such as Kinesis or Pub/Sub are also eliminated because they would lock us with a given provider and we want to be able to host the entire XebiKart infrastructure anywhere (envisionning going multi-cloud). This is also the reason behind the lack of consideration for things such as **AWS IoT** or equivalent MQTT managed solutions.

**Kafka** didn't look like a good candidate for our use-case (also not supporting MQTT), as well as native NATS even if this one is the most ready to be distributed on Kubernetes. **We are still considering both for a later stage** when we will be deeper in the project and when we will be able to split brokers according to their usage.
debate_answer: For a pure IoT focus with low-resource clients, VerneMQ is likely the best fit due to its native MQTT support and scalability. For high-throughput streaming, Kafka is the superior choice, provided the team can manage its complexity. If simplicity and ease of deployment are paramount, NATS offers a lightweight alternative. For leveraging cloud infrastructure, AWS Kinesis or Google Cloud Pub/Sub are excellent for high-throughput needs, assuming the team is comfortable with cloud integration.
reason: The negative side provides a more nuanced analysis of the specific needs of IoT projects, emphasizing the importance of scalability and performance for low-resource clients. It highlights VerneMQ's suitability for IoT due to its native MQTT support and scalability, and Kafka's unmatched capability for high-throughput streaming, which aligns well with the decision drivers. The negative side also addresses the potential overhead of RabbitMQ's architecture and the complexity of managing Kafka, offering a balanced view of the trade-offs involved.
message_history:
To determine the best message broker and protocol for your needs, let's evaluate each option based on the decision drivers provided:

1. **RabbitMQ with MQTT Plugin**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: RabbitMQ with the MQTT plugin is a strong candidate for IoT projects. MQTT is a lightweight protocol designed for low-resource clients, making it suitable for IoT devices.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: While RabbitMQ can handle binary data, it might not be the most efficient for high-throughput streaming of large data like images or video frames.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: RabbitMQ is well-supported on Kubernetes, with Helm charts available for easy deployment.
   - **Existing Knowledge**: If the team already has experience with RabbitMQ, this could be a significant advantage.

2. **VerneMQ**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: VerneMQ is a high-performance MQTT broker, specifically designed for IoT applications, making it a strong candidate for low-resource clients.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: Like RabbitMQ, VerneMQ can handle binary data, but MQTT might not be the best for high-throughput image streaming.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: VerneMQ can be deployed on Kubernetes, though it might require more configuration than RabbitMQ.
   - **Existing Knowledge**: If the team is familiar with MQTT, VerneMQ could be a good fit.

3. **Kafka**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: Kafka is not typically used for low-resource IoT devices due to its heavier protocol, but it excels in high-throughput scenarios.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: Kafka is well-suited for streaming large volumes of data, including images and video frames.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: Kafka can be complex to deploy and manage on Kubernetes, but tools like Strimzi can help.
   - **Existing Knowledge**: If the team has experience with Kafka, it could be a viable option.

4. **NATS**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: NATS is lightweight and can be suitable for IoT applications, though it lacks some of the features of MQTT.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: NATS can handle binary data, but it might not be the best choice for high-throughput image streaming.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: NATS is easy to deploy on Kubernetes and is known for its simplicity.
   - **Existing Knowledge**: If the team is familiar with NATS, it could be a good option.

5. **AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: These are cloud-native solutions that can handle IoT data, but they might not be ideal for low-resource clients due to potential latency and cost.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: Both are capable of handling high-throughput data streams, including images and video.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: As managed services, they don't require deployment on Kubernetes, but integration can be complex.
   - **Existing Knowledge**: If the team has experience with these cloud services, they could be considered.

**Conclusion**:
- If the primary focus is on IoT with low-resource clients and existing team knowledge, **RabbitMQ with MQTT plugin** or **VerneMQ** are strong candidates.
- If high-throughput streaming of images is a priority, and the team can handle the complexity, **Kafka** might be the best choice.
- For simplicity and ease of deployment, **NATS** could be considered if the team is comfortable with its limitations.
- If leveraging cloud services is an option, and the team is familiar with them, **AWS Kinesis** or **Google Cloud Pub/Sub** could be viable, especially for high-throughput needs.
While your analysis is comprehensive, I would like to offer a slightly different perspective, particularly focusing on the decision drivers and the specific needs of IoT projects.

1. **RabbitMQ with MQTT Plugin**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: While RabbitMQ with MQTT is a viable option for IoT, it may not be the most optimized for high-scale IoT environments. MQTT is indeed lightweight, but RabbitMQ's architecture might introduce overhead that could be avoided with a more native MQTT solution.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: RabbitMQ is not inherently designed for high-throughput streaming, which could be a bottleneck for image or video data.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: RabbitMQ is relatively easy to deploy, but managing scaling and performance tuning can be complex.

2. **VerneMQ**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: VerneMQ is specifically designed for MQTT and IoT, making it a more natural fit for low-resource clients. It offers excellent scalability and performance for IoT use cases.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: While MQTT is not typically used for high-throughput streaming, VerneMQ's performance optimizations could make it more suitable than RabbitMQ for this purpose.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: VerneMQ can be more challenging to configure initially, but it offers robust performance once set up.

3. **Kafka**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: Kafka is not ideal for low-resource IoT devices due to its complexity and resource requirements. However, it excels in scenarios where high throughput and data durability are critical.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: Kafka is unmatched in handling large-scale data streams, making it the best choice for streaming images or video frames.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: Kafka's deployment can be complex, but tools like Strimzi simplify this process significantly.

4. **NATS**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: NATS is lightweight and simple, making it a good choice for IoT applications where simplicity and speed are prioritized over advanced features.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: NATS might struggle with high-throughput image streaming compared to Kafka.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: NATS is straightforward to deploy and manage, which is a significant advantage.

5. **AWS Kinesis and Google Cloud Pub/Sub**:
   - **Applicability for IoT**: These services are excellent for handling large-scale data but may not be ideal for low-resource IoT devices due to potential latency and cost.
   - **Streaming Frames/Images**: Both services are capable of handling high-throughput data streams effectively.
   - **Ease of Deployment on Kubernetes**: As managed services, they offer ease of use but require integration efforts.

**Conclusion**:
- For a pure IoT focus with low-resource clients, **VerneMQ** is likely the best fit due to its native MQTT support and scalability.
- For high-throughput streaming, **Kafka** is the superior choice, provided the team can manage its complexity.
- If simplicity and ease of deployment are paramount, **NATS** offers a lightweight alternative.
- For leveraging cloud infrastructure, **AWS Kinesis** or **Google Cloud Pub/Sub** are excellent for high-throughput needs, assuming the team is comfortable with cloud integration.

Ultimately, the choice should align with the team's existing expertise and the specific requirements of the project, balancing between IoT suitability and data streaming capabilities.

## Context and Problem Statement

A solution for isolating third-party payment widgets from the rest of account
management can include building a separate web app on its own dedicated domain.
As a side effect in building that app, we have the opportunity to choose
technologies for building it that don't necessarily follow the rest of FxA.

## Decision Drivers

* Opportunity for a fresh start with tech stack without rewriting
* Security in dealing with payment transactions
* Developer ergonomics
* Code quality & testing
* Subscription services deadlines

## Considered Options

* React, Redux, Typescript
* Backbone.js
human_decision:
# Use React, Redux, and Typescript for subscription management pages

* Deciders: Ben Bangert, Ian Bicking, Les Orchard
* Date: 2019-05-08

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "React, Redux, Typescript", because

* Chance to start with a fresh stack
* More vibrant ecosystem
* Better tooling & developer ergonomics

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### React, Redux, TypeScript

[React][] is a library for building DOM-based UI components in a data-driven,
declarative style that minimizes granular DOM manipulation by developers by way
of a Virtual DOM diff / patch algorithm. It also introduces patterns & tools for
encapsulating reusable UI functionality in "hooks" and JS classes.

[React]: https://reactjs.org/

[Redux][] is a library for managing app state in a singleton container using a
defined inventory of actions that describe changes to be applied via reducer
functions and middleware filters.

[Redux]: https://redux.js.org/

[TypeScript][] is a superset of JavaScript which introduces type annotation
expressions and type inference along with tooling to lint and compile.

[TypeScript]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/

We can also consider other dependencies that act as glue between these pieces
and utilities to reduce boilerplate and repetition. (e.g. [redux-actions][] to
simplify constructing actions; [redux-promise-middleware][] for actions that
result in async API fetches; [redux-thunk][] for producing action sequences)

[redux-actions]: https://redux-actions.js.org/ 

[redux-promise-middleware]:
https://github.com/pburtchaell/redux-promise-middleware 

[redux-thunk]: https://github.com/reduxjs/redux-thunk

* Pros
  * Frequently updated and vibrant ecosystem
  * New team members may be more familiar with the React / Redux / TypeScript
    ecosystem
  * React integrates well with Webpack for code-splitting and lazy-loaded
    component JS bundles
  * React and Webpack can support per-component hot reloading rather than
    reloading the whole page
  * React can organize UI rendering into small components with single
    responsibility for each
  * React hooks can be used to encapsulate reusable chunks of UI logic & state
    between components
  * React offers [safeguards][react-innerhtml] against accidental XSS from
    unescaped HTML.
  * Redux can organize business logic and API requests in a central set of
    modules
  * Data-driven React components can be loosely-coupled with Redux state
  * The Redux dev tools browser add-on can monitor, inspect, and even rewind app
    state changes
  * TypeScript can help document & validate data structures at build time (e.g.
    in Redux state) and integrate with IDEs (e.g. intellisense hints in VS Code)
  * Can follow the Mocha / Chai / Sinon style unit testing stack used elsewhere
    in the project
  * Can use [React bindings][fluent-react] for [fluent.js][fluent] and [FTL][]
    strings for L10n.
* Cons
  * Novel technology stack for the project in general.
  * Fast-moving tech stack that may need chasing after.
  * Reduced code sharing with the rest of the project, reinventing some wheels.
  * Fluent is a research project by Mozilla, [it has occasionally presented
    challenges][fluent-challenges].
  
[fluent]: https://projectfluent.org/
[fluent-react]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent.js/wiki/React-Bindings
[ftl]: https://projectfluent.org/fluent/guide/
[fluent-challenges]: https://github.com/mozilla/testpilot/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed+fluent
[react-innerhtml]: https://reactjs.org/docs/dom-elements.html#dangerouslysetinnerhtml

### Backbone.js

[Backbone.js][] is small framework that offers base View and Model abstractions.

[Backbone.js]: https://backbonejs.org/

FxA combines it with a few other utilities including [Mustache][] for HTML
templates; [Cocktail][] for class mixins; [jQuery][] for DOM manipulation.
Depends on [Underscore.js][] for general data manipulation.

[Mustache]: https://mustache.github.io/ 

[Cocktail]: https://github.com/onsi/cocktail

[jQuery]: https://jquery.com/

[Underscore.js]: https://underscorejs.org/

* Pros
  * Framework of record for client-side code in FxA so far.
  * Granular DOM updates in View classes do not rely on an abstract Virtual DOM
    that can have an impact on performance.
  * Pretty small framework, even with the added utilities.
  * Mature framework that doesn't need much chasing after.
  * Lots of mature tested code within FxA to reuse and on which to model new
    additions.
  * Uses [mature gettext tools & processes for L10n][fxa-l10n].
  * Mustache and Backbone best practices help guard against XSS via unescaped HTML.
* Cons
  * New team members may not be familiar with Backbone patterns
  * Updating Views requires granular DOM changes that can sometime collide
    between different Views that affect the same parts of the DOM - i.e
    overlapping responsibilities.
  * Business logic can be scattered amongst separate Model classes.
  * Infrequent releases: v1.4.0 released on 2019-02, but v1.3.3 released on
    2016-04.
  * fxa-content-server uses v1.1.1 from 2014-02 along with similarly-aged
    versions of Cocktail and Mustache.
  * Fluent [was created][fluent-created] to address issues [found over years of using
    gettext][fluent-v-gettext] at Mozilla.

[fxa-l10n]: https://github.com/mozilla/fxa-content-server-l10n/
[fluent-created]: https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/04/fluent-1-0-a-localization-system-for-natural-sounding-translations/
[fluent-v-gettext]: https://github.com/projectfluent/fluent/wiki/Fluent-vs-gettext
debate_answer: React, Redux, and Typescript are the best option for building the separate web app for isolating third-party payment widgets.
reason: The affirmative side presents a strong case for React, Redux, and Typescript by highlighting their modernity, security features, developer ergonomics, and ability to meet subscription service deadlines. These factors align well with the decision drivers, particularly the opportunity for a fresh start with a modern tech stack and the need for high code quality and testing. While the negative side raises valid points about Backbone.js's simplicity and potential for legacy integration, the overall advantages of React, Redux, and Typescript in terms of modern development practices and ecosystem support make them the superior choice for the given context.
message_history:
In considering the best option for building a separate web app for isolating third-party payment widgets, we need to evaluate the two options—React, Redux, Typescript, and Backbone.js—against the decision drivers provided.

### React, Redux, Typescript

1. **Opportunity for a fresh start with tech stack without rewriting**: 
   - React, Redux, and Typescript offer a modern tech stack that is widely adopted and supported. This combination allows for a fresh start with a robust ecosystem and a wealth of resources and community support.

2. **Security in dealing with payment transactions**: 
   - React and Typescript can enhance security by providing strong typing and reducing runtime errors. Additionally, React's component-based architecture can help in isolating and securing different parts of the application.

3. **Developer ergonomics**: 
   - React is known for its developer-friendly nature, with a component-based architecture that simplifies the development process. Redux provides a predictable state management system, and Typescript adds type safety, which can improve developer productivity and reduce bugs.

4. **Code quality & testing**: 
   - The combination of React, Redux, and Typescript supports high code quality through strong typing, modular architecture, and a rich ecosystem of testing tools like Jest and Enzyme.

5. **Subscription services deadlines**: 
   - React's popularity and the availability of numerous libraries and tools can accelerate development, helping to meet tight deadlines.

### Backbone.js

1. **Opportunity for a fresh start with tech stack without rewriting**: 
   - Backbone.js is an older framework and may not provide the same level of modernity and flexibility as React, Redux, and Typescript. It might not be the best choice for a fresh start.

2. **Security in dealing with payment transactions**: 
   - While Backbone.js can be used securely, it lacks the built-in features and community support for security that modern frameworks like React offer.

3. **Developer ergonomics**: 
   - Backbone.js requires more boilerplate code and manual management of views and data, which can be less ergonomic compared to React's declarative approach.

4. **Code quality & testing**: 
   - Backbone.js does not inherently support the same level of code quality and testing tools as React, Redux, and Typescript. It may require additional effort to achieve similar standards.

5. **Subscription services deadlines**: 
   - Given its age and the decline in popularity, finding developers experienced with Backbone.js might be more challenging, potentially impacting the ability to meet deadlines.

### Conclusion

Considering the decision drivers, **React, Redux, and Typescript** emerge as the superior choice. They offer a modern, secure, and developer-friendly tech stack that aligns well with the goals of high code quality, efficient development, and meeting subscription service deadlines. While Backbone.js has its merits, it does not provide the same level of advantages in the current technological landscape.
While the argument for React, Redux, and Typescript is compelling, there are scenarios where Backbone.js might still be a viable option, depending on specific project requirements and constraints. Let's explore some reasons why Backbone.js could be considered:

### Backbone.js

1. **Simplicity and Lightweight**:
   - Backbone.js is a lightweight framework that can be advantageous for smaller applications or projects where simplicity is key. Its minimalistic nature can lead to faster load times and reduced complexity, which might be beneficial for a payment widget that needs to be quick and efficient.

2. **Legacy System Integration**:
   - If the existing system or other parts of the application are already using Backbone.js, it might make sense to continue using it for consistency and ease of integration. This can reduce the overhead of learning and maintaining multiple frameworks.

3. **Proven Stability**:
   - Backbone.js has been around for a long time and has proven stability. For teams that value a tried-and-tested framework with a straightforward approach, Backbone.js can be a reliable choice.

4. **Customization and Flexibility**:
   - Backbone.js provides more freedom to structure the application as desired, without the constraints of a more opinionated framework like React. This can be beneficial for teams that prefer a high degree of customization.

5. **Resource Constraints**:
   - If the team has existing expertise in Backbone.js and limited resources to train or hire developers skilled in React, Redux, and Typescript, it might be more pragmatic to stick with Backbone.js to meet deadlines and budget constraints.

### Conclusion

While React, Redux, and Typescript offer modern features and a robust ecosystem, Backbone.js should not be dismissed outright. It can be a suitable choice for projects that prioritize simplicity, have existing Backbone.js infrastructure, or face resource constraints. Ultimately, the decision should be based on the specific context of the project, including team expertise, project size, and integration needs.

## Context and Problem Statement

In the context of classic services' requirement for contact HMRC and Welsh information links in their footer, facing the
 fact that these links are missing from hmrcStandardFooter, should we add them?

The additional links needed are:

* "Contact", linking to https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/hm-revenue-customs/contact
* "Rhestr o Wasanaethau Cymraeg", linking to https://www.gov.uk/cymraeg

## Decision Drivers

* The need for consistency across HMRC services.
* Our belief that including them is likely to improve the user experience for tax users.
* We can see no good reason for not including them as standard because they are applicable across HMRC services.
* We have a time sensitive opportunity of acting now while teams are in the process of
uplifting their frontend libraries.
* The HMRC design community have been consulted on multiple public Slack channels and two
successive design system working group meetings, with no objections noted.
* Classic services support multiple live services. Not including these links as standard would mean their having to
duplicate these links, and associated English and Welsh content, across tens of repositories.

## Considered Options

* Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter
* Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links
* Do nothing
human_decision:
# Add contact HMRC and Welsh information links to standard footer

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-12-04

Technical Story: PLATUI-854

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter", because this 
will benefit tax users, and we have a unique window of opportunity to act now.

### Positive Consequences

* Tax users have better information provided to them
* Teams do not need to duplicate content and URLs across hundreds of repositories
* We can more easily maintain the content and links in a central repository

### Negative Consequences

* Teams currently using a Welsh link as their language toggle will likely need to switch to using one of the standard components
for language switching e.g. hmrcLanguageSelect.
* Teams already including a contact link manually will need to remove it when upgrading. 

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter

* Good, because it's more likely the additional links will become standard benefiting
users
* Good, because we can standardise the content for English and Welsh
* Good, because hyperlinks can be maintained in a central place and do not have to be corrected in 100s of separate services
* Bad, because it's possible some teams may not want a contact or Welsh link for valid business reasons

### Create an hmrcExtendedFooter component containing the additional links

* Good, because new links will be added consistently for teams adopting it
* Good, because we can standardise the text for English and Welsh
* Good, because the hyperlinks can be maintained in a central repository
* Bad, because uptake unlikely to be as high as making the links standard
* Bad, because teams may become confused about which footer to use, increasing delivery friction

### Do nothing

* Good, because teams have more flexibility
* Bad, because tax users will not benefit from these additional information links
* Bad, because teams may add the additional links inconsistently with different text, different URLs, or 
 in a different order
* Bad, because there is no standardisation of the content in English and Welsh
* Bad, because if changes need to be made, they will need to be made in 100s of individual services rather
than in one library
* Bad, because teams will have to rollback these changes if they adopt a new standard footer with the contact and Welsh links
debate_answer: Add the links to hmrcStandardFooter.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling argument for adding the links to the hmrcStandardFooter, emphasizing the need for consistency, improved user experience, and efficiency by avoiding duplication across repositories. The decision is supported by the lack of objections from the HMRC design community and the current opportunity to implement changes during frontend library updates. While the negative side raises valid points about flexibility and future-proofing, the immediate benefits of consistency and user experience improvements outweigh the potential complexities of creating a separate component.
