## Context and Problem Statement
While developing the new, unified API, there weren't clear guidelines for structuring new API endpoints. The first approach
was introducing two different endpoint prefixes, similar to what is currently done in a regular shop. On the 30th of April,
we have merged a unification of our endpoints in https://github.com/Sylius/Sylius/pull/11397. This move was dictated by 
the fact that we wanted to push unification even further. Depending on the currently logged in user, we had to expose different
data for available entities in both contexts. Besides, it ought to simplify API usage (as it would reduce the number of endpoints)
and simplify the relations between resources. However, this change rose a lot of new issues to solve:

 * Different serialization for different users is not trivial. Some relations between objects are also contextual, so 
admin can see many more links than the regular visitor or shop user. 
 * Endpoints like "Product Show" are resolved differently depending on the context as well. The shop products are determined 
based on the slug(taking into account the current channel), while the administrator is browsing products by code. This
separation blurs the product identifier, and it is not clear which route should be considered as IRI. 
 * the split was not complete. Two separate log in endpoints have to be preserved, due to our user architecture and the
decision that we want to be explicit, which user is trying to log in.
 
Using one unified identifier in both admin and shop panel is a no go as well. In the shop we should use a `slug`, to be able
to expose product data based on URL (or provide redirect to proper resource based on the channel and locale), while in admin 
resources are channel and locale agnostic, and we should use `codes` instead.

## Decision Drivers

* Driver 1: There is not strict requirement to have a direct 1 to 1 correlation between entities in database and exposed API Resources.
One resource in the database may aggregate several API Resources
* Driver 2: There should be a clear way to determine an IRI for each of exposed API Resources
* Driver 3: A clear separation of available resources and paths for visitor and admin may be expected

## Considered Options

Every option will be described considering products example fetched by shop user and administrator:
human_decision:
# Separation of admin and shop routes

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2020-09-31

### Staying with the current solution

`GET /new-api/products/knitted-wool-blend-green-cap` for Visitor (using product slug) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {} }`
`GET /new-api/products/KNITTED_WOOL_BLEND_GREEN_CAP` for admin user (using product code) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {}, "admin_related_information" : "" }`

* Good, because there is only one endpoint for the products.
* Bad, because depending on the currently logged in user, the IRIs between related products (or on product list) has be
changed (between slug and code).
* Bad, because it is not clear how to operate on endpoint before jumping into documentation. (For example tax rates 
endpoint is not exposed for shop users).
* Bad, because we have to declare authorization rules on endpoint basis, while some of them will have really complicated 
rules (different strategies for admin, shop user and guest)

### Adding additional suffix for path

`GET /new-api/products/knitted-wool-blend-green-cap` for Visitor (using product slug) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {} }`
`GET /new-api/products/KNITTED_WOOL_BLEND_GREEN_CAP/admin` for admin user (using product code) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {}, "admin_related_information" : "" }`

* Good, because we can treat two different presentations as two separate resources and generate two different IRIs for both of them.
* Good, because it is clear which endpoint should be used in admin and shop contexts.
* Good, because the authorization of admin and shop users is more straightforward.
* Bad, because there are two endpoints for the products.
* Bad, because it results with n paths with `admin` prefix only, while on others, we should be cautious which path we are using.
* Bad, because we have to declare authorization rules on endpoint basis, while some of them will have really complicated 
rules (different strategies for admin, shop user, and guest)

### Moving back to prefixed paths for back-office and shop 

`GET /new-api/shop/products/knitted-wool-blend-green-cap` for Visitor (using product slug) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {} }`
`GET /new-api/admin/products/KNITTED_WOOL_BLEND_GREEN_CAP` for admin user (using product code) which will return
`{"id": 1, "slug": 2, "prices": {}, "admin_related_information" : "" }`

* Good, because we can treat two different presentations as two separate resources and generate two different IRIs for both of them.
* Good, because it is clear which endpoint should be used in admin and shop contexts.
* Good, because the authorization of admin and shop users is more straightforward.
* Good, because we can take advantage of Symfony firewalls and hide all admin endpoints behind one firewall. 
* Bad, because there are two endpoints for the products.

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Moving back to prefixed paths for back-office and shop". This option was selected because it allows us to
easily leverage the Symfony Security component's benefits like firewalls and ACL. The only second and third option provides
predictable behavior and clear separation of concerns for admin and shop. Still, suffixes on most of the routes seem like
a worse solution compared to the common prefix. The common prefix will indicate that these endpoints are grouped in the 
same context.
debate_answer: Option 2: Separate Endpoints for Shop and Admin is the best choice.
reason: The negative side provides a compelling argument for Option 2, emphasizing clear separation of concerns, simplified logic, ease of maintenance, enhanced security, and clarity for developers. These factors align well with the decision drivers, particularly Driver 3, which expects a clear separation of resources and paths for different user roles. While the affirmative side's Option 3 offers a balanced approach, it introduces complexity that may not be necessary given the benefits of distinct endpoints. Therefore, Option 2 is the more robust and clear solution.
message_history:
To address the problem statement and decision drivers, we need to consider options that balance the need for unification with the distinct requirements of different user roles (shop users and administrators). Here are some potential options:

### Option 1: Contextual Endpoints with Role-Based Serialization
- **Description**: Maintain a unified endpoint structure but implement role-based serialization. This means that the same endpoint can return different data structures based on the user's role (shop user or admin).
- **Pros**:
  - Simplifies the endpoint structure by reducing the number of endpoints.
  - Allows for flexibility in data presentation based on user roles.
  - Aligns with Driver 1 by allowing aggregation of resources.
- **Cons**:
  - Increases complexity in serialization logic.
  - May require additional logic to determine the appropriate data to return based on user context.

### Option 2: Separate Endpoints for Shop and Admin
- **Description**: Create distinct endpoints for shop users and administrators, each tailored to their specific needs (e.g., `/shop/products/{slug}` for shop users and `/admin/products/{code}` for admins).
- **Pros**:
  - Clear separation of resources and paths, aligning with Driver 3.
  - Simplifies the logic for determining the IRI, as each endpoint has a clear purpose.
- **Cons**:
  - Increases the number of endpoints, which may complicate API management.
  - May lead to duplication of logic across endpoints.

### Option 3: Hybrid Approach with Unified Base and Contextual Paths
- **Description**: Use a unified base endpoint with contextual paths that differentiate between shop and admin contexts (e.g., `/products/{context}/{identifier}` where `context` is either `shop` or `admin`).
- **Pros**:
  - Provides a unified structure while still allowing for context-specific paths.
  - Reduces endpoint duplication compared to Option 2.
  - Supports clear IRI determination by including context in the path.
- **Cons**:
  - May still require complex logic to handle different contexts within the same base endpoint.
  - Could be confusing if not well-documented.

### Option 4: Use Query Parameters for Context Differentiation
- **Description**: Use query parameters to differentiate between contexts within the same endpoint (e.g., `/products?context=shop&identifier=slug`).
- **Pros**:
  - Minimizes the number of endpoints.
  - Allows for flexible context switching without changing the endpoint structure.
- **Cons**:
  - Can lead to complex query parameter handling.
  - May not provide as clear a separation of resources as distinct endpoints.

### Recommendation
Considering the decision drivers, Option 3 (Hybrid Approach with Unified Base and Contextual Paths) seems to be the most balanced solution. It provides a unified structure while allowing for context-specific differentiation, supports clear IRI determination, and reduces endpoint duplication. However, it is crucial to ensure that the API documentation is clear and comprehensive to avoid confusion among users.
I appreciate the analysis provided, but I would argue that Option 2 (Separate Endpoints for Shop and Admin) is the most suitable choice given the context and decision drivers. Here's why:

### Option 2: Separate Endpoints for Shop and Admin
- **Description**: Create distinct endpoints for shop users and administrators, each tailored to their specific needs (e.g., `/shop/products/{slug}` for shop users and `/admin/products/{code}` for admins).

#### Reasons for Choosing Option 2:

1. **Clear Separation of Concerns**:
   - This option aligns perfectly with Driver 3, which expects a clear separation of available resources and paths for visitors and admins. By having distinct endpoints, we ensure that each user role has a dedicated path that caters to their specific needs and context.

2. **Simplified Logic**:
   - While Option 3 attempts to unify the structure, it introduces complexity in handling different contexts within the same base endpoint. Option 2, on the other hand, simplifies the logic for determining the IRI and the data to be returned, as each endpoint has a clear and distinct purpose.

3. **Ease of Maintenance and Scalability**:
   - Having separate endpoints allows for easier maintenance and scalability. Changes or enhancements specific to either the shop or admin context can be made independently without affecting the other, reducing the risk of introducing bugs or inconsistencies.

4. **Enhanced Security and Access Control**:
   - With distinct endpoints, it becomes easier to implement and manage security measures and access controls tailored to each user role. This can help prevent unauthorized access and ensure that sensitive admin data is not inadvertently exposed to shop users.

5. **Clarity and Predictability**:
   - For developers consuming the API, having separate endpoints provides clarity and predictability. They can easily understand which endpoint to use based on their role, reducing the learning curve and potential for errors.

While Option 2 does increase the number of endpoints, the benefits of clear separation, simplified logic, and enhanced security outweigh the potential drawbacks. Additionally, modern API management tools can help mitigate the complexity of managing multiple endpoints.

In conclusion, Option 2 offers a robust and clear solution that aligns with the decision drivers and addresses the challenges outlined in the problem statement effectively.
{"Whether there is a preference": "Yes", "Supported Side": "Negative", "Reason": "The negative side provides a compelling argument for Option 2, emphasizing clear separation of concerns, simplified logic, ease of maintenance, enhanced security, and clarity for developers. These factors align well with the decision drivers, particularly Driver 3, which expects a clear separation of resources and paths for different user roles. While the affirmative side's Option 3 offers a balanced approach, it introduces complexity that may not be necessary given the benefits of distinct endpoints. Therefore, Option 2 is the more robust and clear solution.", "debate_answer": "Option 2: Separate Endpoints for Shop and Admin is the best choice."}

## Context and Problem Statement

We would like to mark the most trending articles in our feed.
Trending means that the number of views is growing over time.
How should we identify the trending articles in near real-time?

## Decision Drivers

* Easy to build
* No-ops solution

## Considered Options

* Cron job
* Google Dataflow
* Apache Spark
human_decision:
# ADR 4: Trending Articles Solution

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: @idoshamun
* Date: 2020-11-22

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: Cron job, because it utilizes our existing API server and its infrastructure.
This should be the most straightforward solution to implement.

### Positive Consequences

* Utilize existing API
* No-ops needed

### Negative Consequences

* Very limited in terms of possible calculations compared to data processing platforms
* Might create load on the database as we grow
* I believe that in the near future we will have to migrate to a different solution

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Google Dataflow

* Good, because it uses a Apache Beam which is an open source library
* Good, because it's a fully managed with automatic provisioning solution
* Good, because it's a very flexible platform
* Bad, because it will increase our cloud cost

message_history:
To determine the best option for identifying trending articles in near real-time, we need to evaluate each of the considered options against the decision drivers: easy to build and no-ops solution.

1. **Cron Job**:
   - **Easy to Build**: Cron jobs are relatively simple to set up for scheduled tasks. However, they are not inherently designed for real-time processing. They would require additional scripting and logic to handle data collection and analysis, which could complicate the build process.
   - **No-ops Solution**: Cron jobs require ongoing maintenance, such as server management and ensuring the scripts run correctly. This does not align well with a no-ops approach.

2. **Google Dataflow**:
   - **Easy to Build**: Google Dataflow is a fully managed service for stream and batch data processing. It provides a high-level programming model that can simplify the development process, especially for real-time data processing tasks.
   - **No-ops Solution**: As a fully managed service, Google Dataflow handles infrastructure management, scaling, and maintenance, making it a strong candidate for a no-ops solution.

3. **Apache Spark**:
   - **Easy to Build**: Apache Spark is a powerful data processing engine that can handle large-scale data processing tasks. However, setting up and managing a Spark cluster can be complex, especially for real-time processing.
   - **No-ops Solution**: While there are managed Spark services (e.g., Databricks), using Spark typically involves more operational overhead compared to a fully managed service like Google Dataflow.

Considering the decision drivers, Google Dataflow appears to be the best option. It offers a balance of ease of development with its high-level abstractions and a no-ops solution through its fully managed nature. This makes it well-suited for identifying trending articles in near real-time without the need for extensive operational management.
While Google Dataflow is indeed a strong candidate due to its fully managed nature and ease of use for real-time data processing, there are reasons to consider Apache Spark, particularly if certain conditions or preferences exist within the organization.

1. **Apache Spark**:
   - **Easy to Build**: Although setting up Apache Spark can be complex, it offers a robust ecosystem for data processing. If the organization already has expertise in Spark or uses a managed service like Databricks, the complexity can be significantly reduced. Spark's rich library support for machine learning and data processing can be advantageous for more sophisticated trending algorithms.
   - **No-ops Solution**: Managed Spark services like Databricks or AWS Glue can offer a no-ops experience similar to Google Dataflow. These services handle the infrastructure, scaling, and maintenance, allowing developers to focus on building the application logic.

2. **Flexibility and Integration**: Apache Spark provides flexibility in terms of integration with various data sources and sinks. If the organization has a diverse data ecosystem or requires integration with specific tools, Spark's versatility might be beneficial.

3. **Cost Considerations**: Depending on the scale and specific use case, the cost structure of managed Spark services might be more favorable compared to Google Dataflow, especially if the organization can leverage existing cloud credits or agreements.

In conclusion, while Google Dataflow is a strong contender for its ease of use and no-ops nature, Apache Spark should not be dismissed outright. If the organization has existing Spark expertise, requires specific integrations, or finds the cost structure more favorable, Spark could be a viable option. Ultimately, the decision should consider the organization's existing infrastructure, expertise, and specific requirements for identifying trending articles.

## Context

The decision was made in [ADR 21](https://github.com/wmde/fundraising-application/blob/main/doc/adr/021_Single_or_Multi_Page_Application_Architecture.md) to move towards an API and Single-Page-Application. This document covers the decisions made around the design of the API.

## Decision Drivers
* Maintainability - The API should be easy to maintain.
* Consistency - The API controllers should be consistent in how they handle requests and send responses.
* Accessibility - The API should be well documented.

## Considered Options
We considered 3 options:
* Build it ourselves.
* The [Symfony Json bundle](https://github.com/symfony-bundles/json-request-bundle).
* The [FOSRest Bundle](https://github.com/FriendsOfSymfony/FOSRestBundle).

The Json bundle combines body items into the `request->get()` bucket making the JSON body data indistinguishable from query parameters which rules it out.

The FOSRest Bundle is too big, and very integrated into Symfony, which means it's not orthogonal enough for our requirements.

We decided to build it ourselves. This has the advantage of keeping it concise, and as long as we follow our design rules it will be consistent and maintainable.
human_decision:
# Fundraising Application API Design

Date: 2021-11-30

Deciders: Gabriel Birke, Corinna Hillebrandt, Abban Dunne

Technical Story:

## Status

Accepted

## Decisions
* We will use Swagger for the API documentation. This will eventually be the source of truth.
* We will eventually use the Swagger yaml files to generate automated tests.
* The API URL schema should be REST-ish, and use http verbs and resource controllers for performing actions on resources.
* Symphony has no resource controllers, instead we follow the [Laravel style guide](https://laravel.com/docs/8.x/controllers#actions-handled-by-resource-controller) for controller [method naming](#controller-actions).
* Response Status' are HTTP codes.
* Responses and Requests are json.
* POST/PUT requests contain the data as a json object in the Request body.
* Responses also return json in the body. 
* API response messages are verbose snake_case strings. This means they can be read by humans and machines.
* UseCases should return data objects and not domain entities.
* Entity create and save should return the updated entity in the response.
* Keys in the responses are camelCase. This allows us to serialise our php data entities without having to transform the property names into snake_case.

### Controller Actions

| HTTP Method | URL | Controller Method |
|:--|:--|:--|
| GET | `/entity` | `index()` |
| POST | `/entity` | `store()` |
| GET | `/entity/{id}` | `show()` |
| PUT | `/entity` | `update()` |
| DELETE | `/entity/{id}` | `destroy()` |

## Links
* https://github.com/NationalBankBelgium/REST-API-Design-Guide
* https://swagger.io/
* https://laravel.com/docs/8.x/controllers#actions-handled-by-resource-controller
debate_answer: Building the API ourselves is the best option given the context and decision drivers.
reason: The affirmative side provides a compelling argument that aligns with the decision drivers of maintainability, consistency, and accessibility. Building the API in-house allows for greater control over the codebase, ensuring that it meets specific needs and standards. The negative side raises valid concerns about resource allocation and the benefits of proven solutions, but the context suggests that the specific drawbacks of the third-party options outweigh these concerns. The decision to build the API ourselves is supported by the need for a tailored, consistent, and maintainable solution.
message_history:
Based on the context and decision drivers provided, the decision to build the API ourselves seems to be the best option. Let's break down the reasoning:

1. **Maintainability**: Building the API in-house allows for greater control over the codebase. The team can ensure that the API is designed in a way that aligns with their specific needs and standards, making it easier to maintain over time. Custom solutions can be tailored to fit the existing architecture and development practices, reducing the complexity that might come with integrating third-party solutions.

2. **Consistency**: By developing the API internally, the team can ensure that all controllers handle requests and responses in a consistent manner. This consistency is crucial for both developers working on the API and those consuming it, as it reduces the learning curve and potential for errors.

3. **Accessibility**: With a custom-built API, the team can ensure that documentation is comprehensive and tailored to the specific implementation. This can improve accessibility for developers who need to understand and work with the API.

The other options, the Symfony Json bundle and the FOSRest Bundle, were ruled out for specific reasons:

- The Symfony Json bundle was not suitable because it combines body items into the `request->get()` bucket, making JSON body data indistinguishable from query parameters. This could lead to confusion and errors in handling requests.

- The FOSRest Bundle was considered too large and too integrated into Symfony, which could introduce unnecessary complexity and reduce the orthogonality of the solution. This could make the API harder to maintain and less flexible.

In conclusion, building the API in-house aligns well with the decision drivers of maintainability, consistency, and accessibility, making it the best option given the context.
While building the API in-house has its advantages, there are also potential drawbacks that should be considered. Let's explore why one might disagree with the decision to build the API ourselves:

1. **Resource Allocation**: Developing an API from scratch can be resource-intensive. It requires significant time and effort from the development team, which could be spent on other critical tasks. By using a third-party solution, the team could leverage existing, well-tested functionalities and focus on other areas of the project.

2. **Proven Solutions**: Third-party bundles like the Symfony Json bundle and the FOSRest Bundle are widely used and have been tested in various scenarios. They come with community support and regular updates, which can be beneficial for long-term maintenance and security. Building an API in-house means the team is responsible for all updates, bug fixes, and security patches.

3. **Scalability and Features**: Established bundles often come with a range of features that might not be immediately necessary but could be beneficial as the project grows. By building the API ourselves, we might miss out on these features or have to invest additional time to develop them later.

4. **Risk of Reinventing the Wheel**: There is a risk of duplicating efforts by building something that already exists. This can lead to unnecessary complexity and potential oversights in the design and implementation phases.

5. **Integration and Compatibility**: While the FOSRest Bundle was considered too integrated into Symfony, this integration can also be seen as a benefit. It ensures compatibility and can simplify the development process by adhering to Symfony's standards and practices.

In conclusion, while building the API in-house offers control and customization, it also comes with challenges related to resource allocation, scalability, and long-term maintenance. Depending on the team's capacity and priorities, leveraging a third-party solution might be a more efficient and sustainable choice.

## Context and Problem Statement

We want to use a state of the art database that can store services/applications according to our data Model and is easy to integrate with [Spring Boot](0001-java-framework.md). 


## Decision Drivers

* Integration with [Spring Boot](0001-java-framework.md)
* Different versions of a service will be stored as individual services
* Applications will be seen  as concrete instances of services

## Considered Options

* Relational database:
    * __MySQL__
    * MariaDB
    * PostgreSQL
    * SQlite
* Graph database:
    * Neo4J

debate_answer: PostgreSQL
reason: Given the context and problem statement, the decision drivers emphasize the need for integration with Spring Boot, handling different versions of services, and viewing applications as concrete instances of services. PostgreSQL is a strong candidate due to its advanced features, robust data integrity, and excellent integration with Spring Boot. It can efficiently manage different versions of services and handle complex data models. Neo4J is also a viable option if the data model involves complex relationships, but PostgreSQL's versatility and strong community support make it a more balanced choice for a wide range of applications.
message_history:
When selecting the best database option for storing services/applications according to the given data model and ensuring easy integration with Spring Boot, we need to consider several factors based on the decision drivers.

### Relational Databases

1. **MySQL**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: MySQL is widely used and has excellent support for Spring Boot through Spring Data JPA. It is a mature and stable option with a large community and extensive documentation.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: MySQL can handle different versions of a service as individual records, but it may require additional schema design to efficiently manage versioning and instances.

2. **MariaDB**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: MariaDB is a fork of MySQL and is fully compatible with it. It offers similar integration capabilities with Spring Boot.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: Like MySQL, it can manage different versions and instances but may require additional schema considerations.

3. **PostgreSQL**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: PostgreSQL is known for its advanced features and strong support for Spring Boot. It offers robust data integrity and complex querying capabilities.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: PostgreSQL's support for JSONB and other advanced data types can be advantageous for handling versioning and instances.

4. **SQLite**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: SQLite is lightweight and easy to set up, but it may not be the best choice for production environments where scalability and concurrency are concerns.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: It can handle versioning and instances but is more suited for smaller applications or development environments.

### Graph Database

1. **Neo4J**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: Neo4J has good integration with Spring Boot through Spring Data Neo4J. It is particularly well-suited for applications with complex relationships and graph-based queries.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: Neo4J excels in scenarios where relationships between entities are complex, making it a strong candidate if the data model involves intricate connections between services and applications.

### Conclusion

- If the primary concern is traditional relational data management with strong community support and advanced features, **PostgreSQL** is a compelling choice due to its robust capabilities and excellent Spring Boot integration.
- If the data model involves complex relationships and the application can benefit from graph-based querying, **Neo4J** is a strong contender, especially if the relationships between services and applications are a focal point.

Ultimately, the choice depends on the specific requirements of the data model and the complexity of relationships between services and applications. If relational data management suffices, PostgreSQL is a solid choice. If graph-based relationships are central, Neo4J may be more appropriate.
While I understand the reasoning behind selecting PostgreSQL or Neo4J based on the context and decision drivers, I would like to present an alternative perspective that might favor a different choice.

### Alternative Perspective

1. **MySQL**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: MySQL's integration with Spring Boot is seamless, thanks to Spring Data JPA. It is a well-established database with a vast ecosystem and community support.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: MySQL can efficiently handle versioning and instances with proper schema design. It offers features like partitioning and indexing that can optimize performance for storing different versions of services.

2. **MariaDB**:
   - **Integration with Spring Boot**: MariaDB, being a fork of MySQL, shares similar integration capabilities and benefits from additional features and optimizations.
   - **Versioning and Instances**: MariaDB can be a more performant option in certain scenarios due to its enhancements over MySQL, making it a viable choice for handling service versions and instances.

### Why Consider MySQL or MariaDB?

- **Performance and Scalability**: Both MySQL and MariaDB are known for their performance and scalability in handling large datasets, which can be crucial if the application is expected to grow.
- **Cost-Effectiveness**: MySQL and MariaDB are open-source and widely supported, which can be a cost-effective solution for many organizations.
- **Familiarity and Support**: Many developers and organizations are already familiar with MySQL, and it has extensive documentation and community support, making it easier to find solutions to potential issues.

### Conclusion

While PostgreSQL and Neo4J have their strengths, MySQL or MariaDB could be the best choice if the project values performance, scalability, and cost-effectiveness, along with strong community support. They are particularly suitable if the data model does not require the advanced features of PostgreSQL or the graph-based querying capabilities of Neo4J.

Ultimately, the decision should be based on the specific needs of the project, including expected data complexity, growth, and available resources. If the project leans towards a more traditional relational database setup with a focus on performance and scalability, MySQL or MariaDB could be the optimal choice.

## Context and Problem Statement

To deliver BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform, custom
deliverables must be produced to support the serving of the Universal
Viewer, and an underlying API which records audit events and persists
them into a database.

This ADR provides options on technologies and frameworks which can be
used to produce these deliverables.

## Decision Drivers

* Ability for Digirati to rapidly deliver the solution.
* Long-term support and stability of the technology and framework
  choices.
* Ease of deployment across different environments / operating systems.
* Robustness and performance of the solution.
* Any technology and framework should be open source.

## Considered Options

* Kotlin (https://kotlinlang.org/) with Spring (https://spring.io/)
* TypeScript (https://www.typescriptlang.org/) with Express.js
  (https://expressjs.com/)
* Python (https://www.python.org/) with Django
  (https://www.djangoproject.com/)
human_decision:
# Choice of Technology and Framework

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: Daniel Grant <daniel.grant@digirati.com>, Gary Tierney
  <gary.tierney@digirati.com>, Stephen Fraser
  <stephen.fraser@digirati.com>
* Date: 2021-02-16

## Decision Outcome

Kotlin is selected as the solution owing to Digirati's vast experience
with the JVM, and when combined with Spring will make this the
deliverables straightforward.

Kotlin does not have the boilerplate cost associated with Java, and as a
result internal there should be fewer barriers in identifying and
understanding the domain specific code required for the deliverables.

In addition, since Spring handles significant amounts of the heavy
lifting, we do not expect to consume time writing code that is not
concerned entirely with handling these audit events.

### Positive Consequences

* Faster development cycle for the parts of the auditing platform which
  require custom code.
* Spring can provide both the Universal Viewer assets and the API,
  unifying everything under one service and keeping authentication
  simple
* The deployment is straightforward, whether using a containerisation or
  deploying directly onto a bare metal server.

### Negative Consequences

* Lack of JVM / Kotlin experience within internal BFI teams.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Kotlin with Spring

Spring is a well known framework for building applications on JVM
platforms. It has been the industry leader in the Java space for a long
time and as a result is capable of solving the majority of technical
requirements we'd encounter during the delivery of the solution.

Kotlin is a relatively new technology when compared to Java, however it
compiles to bytecode and thus runs on a JVM. As a result it offers
interoperability both ways between Java and Kotlin code. Kotlin's core
strength is the ability to write code unconstrained by the boilerplate
of Java while still having the entire Java ecosystem available as
tooling.

#### Pros

* 20+ years of combined experience with JVM and Spring applications.
* Tooling for the JVM and Kotlin makes developing the solution a
  plug-and-play experience.
* Spring Boot framework suits itself well to rapid development.
* Zero-configuration OAuth 2.0 / OIDC integration available from Spring.
* JVM as a deployment target may make bare-metal deployment easier when
  running existing JVM applications.
* Rich documentation and examples are available.

#### Cons

* Limited / no experience at BFI with writing code for the JVM.

### TypeScript with Express.js

Express.js is a barebones web server implementation for the NodeJS
platform. The web server itself is mostly a skeleton, which is usually
extended using middleware that is able to intercept requests and
responses, reading and writing to them as needed to add additional
functionality. There is a lot of existing middleware for Express.js
developed by the community that could be used to meet the solution
requirements (e.g. session management, OAuth 2.0 / OIDC integration,
etc).

TypeScript is a loosely typed programming language that sits over the
top of JavaScript and adds strict validation over program source code,
similar to a Java codebase. This allows the writing of code that stays
clear and maintainable over time, by using the type system to document
itself. A fully TypeScript codebase requires that dependencies also have
TypeScript bindings available.

#### Pros

* Digirati have experience building lightweight Express.js applications.
* Rapdi development can be achieved through constant recompilation
  (e.g., watch style commands).

#### Cons

* Less of an "out of the box" solution.
* Poor documentation, outdated examples, frequently changing ecosystem.

### Python with Django

Django is a web framework intended for rapid development in Python.
Similar to Spring, it comes with the majority of the tools that are
needed in building a modern web application out of the box which allows
developers to get started writing domain specific code.

#### Pros

* Internal BFI staff have experience with Python.
* Django is built for rapid development through constant recompilation
  (e.g., watch style commands).
* Rich documentation, lots of examples, stable versioning.

#### Cons

* Digirati have more experience in delivering identity and access
  management with JVM languages.
debate_answer: Given the decision drivers, Python with Django might be the best option. It offers a balance of rapid development, long-term stability, and robustness, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. Its ease of use and deployment can also facilitate rapid delivery, especially if the team has Python experience.

This aligns well with the decision drivers outlined in the context.

In evaluating the best option for delivering BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform, we need to consider the decision drivers and how each technology stack aligns with them. Let's break down each option:

1. **Kotlin with Spring:**
   - **Rapid Delivery:** Kotlin, being interoperable with Java, can leverage the vast ecosystem of Java libraries, which might speed up development. Spring is a mature framework with extensive documentation and community support, which can also aid rapid delivery.
   - **Long-term Support and Stability:** Kotlin is backed by JetBrains and has been gaining popularity, especially in Android development. Spring is a well-established framework with a strong track record.
   - **Ease of Deployment:** Spring applications can be containerized and deployed across various environments, but it might require more setup compared to other options.
   - **Robustness and Performance:** Spring is known for its robustness and scalability, making it suitable for enterprise-level applications.
   - **Open Source:** Both Kotlin and Spring are open source.

2. **TypeScript with Express.js:**
   - **Rapid Delivery:** TypeScript offers type safety on top of JavaScript, which can reduce runtime errors and speed up development. Express.js is lightweight and easy to set up, which can facilitate rapid prototyping.
   - **Long-term Support and Stability:** TypeScript is maintained by Microsoft and has a growing community. Express.js is one of the most popular Node.js frameworks with a large ecosystem.
   - **Ease of Deployment:** Node.js applications are easy to deploy across different environments, and the JavaScript ecosystem offers many tools for deployment.
   - **Robustness and Performance:** While Express.js is performant for I/O-bound tasks, it might not be as robust as Spring for complex, large-scale applications.
   - **Open Source:** Both TypeScript and Express.js are open source.

3. **Python with Django:**
   - **Rapid Delivery:** Python is known for its simplicity and readability, which can speed up development. Django is a high-level framework that provides many built-in features, reducing the need for third-party libraries.
   - **Long-term Support and Stability:** Python is a mature language with a large community. Django is a well-established framework with long-term support.
   - **Ease of Deployment:** Django applications can be easily deployed using various tools and platforms, but might require more configuration for scaling.
   - **Robustness and Performance:** Django is robust and suitable for building complex applications, but might not match the raw performance of Node.js or Spring for certain tasks.
   - **Open Source:** Both Python and Django are open source.

**Conclusion:**
- If rapid delivery and ease of deployment are the top priorities, **TypeScript with Express.js** might be the best choice due to its lightweight nature and ease of use.
- If robustness, performance, and long-term stability are more critical, **Kotlin with Spring** could be the better option, especially for enterprise-level requirements.
- **Python with Django** offers a balance of rapid development and robustness, making it a strong contender if the team is familiar with Python.

Ultimately, the decision might also depend on the existing expertise of the development team and the specific requirements of the project.

## Context and Problem Statement

We want a way to define and manage the deployment of resources which are shared across multiple solutions.
A number of services offered by cloud providers, namely AWS have services which can only be deployed once per AWS Account or enable a service across an entire account
How should we manage these resources to scale with our plugin support and to support the features we have introduced for our other deployments

## Decision Drivers

* Support required for multi region deployments to allow global image registry
* Deprecation of the fragment and case style template processing which is currently used for account level deployments

## Considered Options

* Add support for a layer level component like deployment option
* Refactor existing services to use a similar approach to extensions and apply extensions to layers
human_decision:
# Layer Level Deployments

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: roleyfoley
* Date: 2021-01-04

Technical Story: Support for deployments of shared resources across layers defined in hamlet

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Add support for a layer level component like deployment option", because this allows for greater flexibility over the resources we can deploy and aligns with our other deployment processes

### Positive Consequences

* Layer level components work in a similar way to our other components
* We can use placements, providers and plugins to support custom layer deployments
* Extends the existing account level deployments to work across user defined layers and support


### Negative Consequences

* Additional complexity in introducing the state management for these components
* Processing requires additional steps including the creation of an occurrence like structure
* The concept for the layer deployments will be similar to occurrences but won't be exactly the same

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Add support for a layer level component like deployment option

The idea is to introduce a layer based component structure, at the moment components are only available under tiers and we use the components to in turn create occurrences. This allows for users to define instances of components and to define specific types. This would introduce the idea of Foundations which are singleton types defined in a plugin and assigned to a defined layer. Foundations are configured as part of a layers configuration and would store its state based on the layer that it has been assigned to. This allows for the state of foundations to be used by multiple solutions. In this model the foundations for a layer would be defined by a plugin and remove the need for users to know which foundations create dependencies for other components.

So instead of a user being able to deploy multiple instances of a given component type each foundation type can only be deployed once, foundations can optionally offer to be disabled or enabled as part of their configuration

* Good, because it aligns with our existing concepts around distinct deployment units which are standalone deployments
* Good, because it is similar to own component style deployment process
* Good, because we can use standardised naming and state querying ( resources, attributes etc. )
* Bad, because it adds complexity in our processing and requires the implementation of a new occurrence structure ( one which supports singleton deployments )


### Refactor existing services to use a similar approach to extensions and apply extensions to layers

In this approach add extension support to layers, in this approach each layer would have a single deployment unit and the configured extensions on the layer would determine what is created for a given deployment. The state would then be provided in a similar way that we do now. In this approach the user would need to define the layers they require and have knowledge of required and optional deployment resources

* Good, because it is simple and simplifies the management of layer based deployments, i.e. add the layer level extensions you want and deploy
* Good, because it aligns with the current approach and would require less work to implement
* Bad, because it wouldn't gain the benefits of state sharing and would require global naming functions to support
* Bad, because it could create conflicting resources as part of a shared single deployment
debate_answer: Add support for a layer level component like deployment option.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for adopting a layer level component like deployment option, emphasizing its long-term benefits in terms of modularity, scalability, and future-proofing the deployment strategy. While it acknowledges the initial complexity, it argues that the investment will lead to a more robust and adaptable system, which aligns with the need for a modern and scalable architecture. This approach seems better suited to accommodate future changes and innovations, addressing the decision drivers more comprehensively.
message_history:
In considering the best option for managing shared resources across multiple solutions, particularly in the context of AWS services that are limited to a single deployment per account, we need to weigh the decision drivers and the considered options carefully.

### Decision Drivers Analysis

1. **Support for Multi-Region Deployments**: This is crucial for ensuring that resources like a global image registry are accessible and consistent across different geographical locations. The solution must facilitate seamless deployment and management across multiple regions.

2. **Deprecation of Fragment and Case Style Template Processing**: The current method of using fragment and case style templates is being deprecated, which means the new solution must not rely on these outdated methods. This requires a more modern and flexible approach to deployment.

### Considered Options

1. **Add Support for a Layer Level Component Like Deployment Option**:
   - **Pros**: This approach could provide a more modular and scalable way to manage shared resources. By defining components at a layer level, it allows for better organization and potentially easier management of resources that need to be deployed once per account.
   - **Cons**: Implementing a new layer level component system might require significant changes to existing infrastructure and could introduce complexity in managing dependencies between layers.

2. **Refactor Existing Services to Use a Similar Approach to Extensions and Apply Extensions to Layers**:
   - **Pros**: This option leverages existing infrastructure and methodologies, which could make the transition smoother. By using extensions, it allows for more flexibility and reusability of components across different deployments.
   - **Cons**: Refactoring existing services can be time-consuming and may require a thorough understanding of current systems to avoid introducing errors. It might also be limited by the capabilities of the current extension system.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers, particularly the need for multi-region support and moving away from deprecated template processing, the second option—refactoring existing services to use a similar approach to extensions and apply extensions to layers—seems to be the more viable choice. This option aligns with the need for flexibility and reusability, which are critical for scaling deployments across multiple regions. It also leverages existing systems, potentially reducing the time and resources needed for implementation compared to introducing a completely new layer level component system.

However, it's important to ensure that the extension system is robust enough to handle the complexities of account-level resource management and that the refactoring process is carefully planned to minimize disruptions.
Certainly, let's explore the alternative perspective.

### Decision Drivers Analysis

1. **Support for Multi-Region Deployments**: The need for a global image registry and consistent resource management across regions is paramount. The solution must inherently support scalability and flexibility across different geographical locations.

2. **Deprecation of Fragment and Case Style Template Processing**: Moving away from deprecated methods necessitates a forward-thinking approach that can accommodate future changes and innovations in deployment strategies.

### Considered Options

1. **Add Support for a Layer Level Component Like Deployment Option**:
   - **Pros**: This approach introduces a new paradigm that could inherently support modularity and scalability. By defining resources at a layer level, it allows for a clear separation of concerns, making it easier to manage and deploy resources that are shared across multiple solutions. This could also facilitate better integration with new technologies and methodologies as they emerge.
   - **Cons**: While it may require significant changes to existing infrastructure, this investment could pay off in the long run by providing a more robust and adaptable system. The initial complexity might be mitigated by the long-term benefits of having a more organized and scalable architecture.

2. **Refactor Existing Services to Use a Similar Approach to Extensions and Apply Extensions to Layers**:
   - **Pros**: Leveraging existing infrastructure can indeed make the transition smoother and less resource-intensive initially. It allows for incremental changes and can be less disruptive in the short term.
   - **Cons**: This approach might be constrained by the limitations of the current extension system. It could also lead to a patchwork solution that might not fully address the need for a modern, scalable deployment strategy. Over time, this could result in technical debt and require further refactoring.

### Conclusion

While refactoring existing services to use extensions might seem like a less disruptive choice initially, the long-term benefits of adding support for a layer level component like deployment option could outweigh the initial challenges. This approach offers a more future-proof solution by providing a clear, modular, and scalable architecture that can better accommodate multi-region deployments and adapt to future changes in deployment strategies.

By investing in a new layer level component system, organizations can ensure that their deployment strategy is not only aligned with current needs but is also prepared for future growth and technological advancements. This proactive approach could ultimately lead to more efficient resource management and a more resilient infrastructure.

## Context and Problem Statement

We'd like to change the spacing system to something more generic.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

The current pattern of relative spacing (half, double) that's based on a default, is deficient in a couple of ways:

- We'd like to add additional spacing values that don't map cleanly to these words. If default spacing is 16, there's no clean word to describe the jump to 24.
- The current model makes it impossible to ever cleanly change the default value. Given the model of default, half, and double, if we decided to make double the new default, the current default would have to be renamed to half, and half would need to be renamed to half/half.

## Considered Options

- Encode the value directly in the class. padding-16 / margin-x-32
- Use generic numbering system (padding-2 / margin-x-4)
- Use generic tshirt system (padding-s / margin-x-l)
human_decision:
# Utilize relative spacing through tshirt sizes

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Deciders:** Rey Wright, Adam Detrick
- **Date:** 2020-08-28

---

## Decision Outcome

Generic tshirt system was chosen over the other options.

- Coding a direct "arbitrary value" into a CSS class (color, number representing a unit) is generally considered an anti-pattern for reasons that are too long to cover in this ADR.
- We like the numbering system but we use t-shirt sizes everywhere else in our system so it makes sense to skew towards alignment with the larger system.

## Positive Consequences <!-- optional -->

- By doing this we were able to add additional spacing classes without any friction and we'll be able to add / remove these easily in the future.
- It's very easy to change what our default spacing is now if we so choose.

## Negative Consequences <!-- optional -->

- The jumps are arbitrary and it's not always immediately clear to an engineer how a relative jump maps to the raw spacing value.
debate_answer: The t-shirt sizing system (padding-s / margin-x-l) is the best option for changing the spacing system to something more generic.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for the t-shirt sizing system, emphasizing its intuitiveness, ease of understanding, and alignment with existing design systems. These factors make it a user-friendly and consistent approach, which is crucial for maintaining a cohesive design language. The t-shirt system also offers scalability with clarity, which is a significant advantage over the more abstract generic numbering system.
message_history:
In considering the best option for changing the spacing system to something more generic, we need to evaluate each of the proposed options based on flexibility, scalability, and ease of understanding.

1. **Encode the value directly in the class (padding-16 / margin-x-32):**
   - **Pros:** This method is explicit and clear, as it directly states the spacing value. It allows for precise control and easy adjustments without ambiguity. It also scales well since you can add any value without needing to fit it into a predefined category.
   - **Cons:** It can lead to a lack of consistency if not managed properly, as developers might use arbitrary values. It also requires knowledge of the specific values used, which might not be intuitive for all team members.

2. **Use a generic numbering system (padding-2 / margin-x-4):**
   - **Pros:** This system is more abstract and can be easily scaled. It provides a consistent framework where each number corresponds to a specific spacing value, which can be documented and understood across the team.
   - **Cons:** It requires a mapping system to understand what each number represents, which can add a layer of complexity. It might not be as intuitive as directly encoding the value.

3. **Use a generic t-shirt system (padding-s / margin-x-l):**
   - **Pros:** This method is intuitive and easy to understand, as it uses familiar sizing terms (small, medium, large). It provides a good balance between abstraction and clarity, making it easy for team members to grasp the relative sizes.
   - **Cons:** It might not be as flexible as the other options when it comes to adding new, non-standard sizes. The system could become cumbersome if too many sizes are needed, as it might not be clear how to represent them.

**Conclusion:**

The best option depends on the specific needs and context of the team. However, considering the need for a more generic and scalable system, the **generic numbering system** (padding-2 / margin-x-4) seems to be the most balanced choice. It offers a structured approach that can accommodate a wide range of values while maintaining consistency. It also allows for easy adjustments and scalability, which are crucial given the problem statement's emphasis on flexibility and the ability to change default values.
While the generic numbering system offers a structured approach, I would argue that the **t-shirt sizing system** (padding-s / margin-x-l) is the best option for changing the spacing system to something more generic. Here's why:

1. **Intuitiveness and Ease of Understanding:**
   - The t-shirt sizing system uses familiar terms like small, medium, and large, which are immediately understandable to most people. This reduces the learning curve for new team members and makes it easier for non-developers to understand and communicate design specifications.

2. **Consistency and Simplicity:**
   - By using a limited set of predefined sizes, the t-shirt system encourages consistency across the design. It prevents the arbitrary use of spacing values, which can happen with a system that encodes values directly in the class. This consistency is crucial for maintaining a cohesive design language.

3. **Flexibility with a Controlled Range:**
   - While it might seem less flexible, the t-shirt system can be extended with additional sizes (e.g., XS, XL) if needed. This allows for a controlled expansion of the system without overwhelming users with too many options. It strikes a balance between flexibility and simplicity.

4. **Alignment with Design Systems:**
   - Many design systems and frameworks already use t-shirt sizing for various components, making it a familiar approach for designers and developers. This alignment can facilitate smoother integration with existing tools and practices.

5. **Scalability with Clarity:**
   - Although the numbering system is scalable, it can become abstract and require additional documentation to map numbers to specific values. The t-shirt system, on the other hand, scales with clarity, as each size inherently conveys a relative magnitude.

In conclusion, the t-shirt sizing system offers a user-friendly, consistent, and scalable approach that aligns well with design practices. It provides enough flexibility to accommodate various spacing needs while maintaining simplicity and clarity, making it the best option for a more generic spacing system.

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "MADR", because it was the only option considered, but also because we're already "all in" on Markdown documentation and GitHub. This is well timed given the Decision Drivers above.
debate_answer: The MADR format, combined with Jekyll for static site generation and GitHub for hosting and workflow, is the best option for making architectural decisions in a collaborative, transparent, and organized manner.
reason: The affirmative side effectively addresses the criteria outlined in the problem statement, emphasizing the structured, collaborative, and transparent nature of the MADR format. It highlights how MADR meets the needs for being broad-based, contextualized, versionable, transparent, centralized, and discoverable. While the negative side raises valid concerns about complexity, overhead, and accessibility, the affirmative side's argument aligns more closely with the specific requirements and goals outlined in the context and problem statement.
message_history:
The context and problem statement you've provided highlight the need for a structured, collaborative, and transparent approach to making architectural decisions within a team. Given the criteria outlined—broad-based, contextualized, versionable, transparent, centralized, and discoverable—the MADR (Markdown Architectural Decision Records) format seems to be a strong candidate. Let's explore why this might be the best option:

1. **Broad-based**: MADR encourages collaboration by allowing multiple team members to contribute to the decision-making process. The use of GitHub pull requests for discussion and approval ensures that decisions are made collectively, with input from various stakeholders.

2. **Contextualized**: MADR provides a structured format for documenting decisions, including the rationale and alternative options considered. This ensures that each decision is well-documented and can be understood in context.

3. **Versionable**: By using GitHub for hosting, MADR takes advantage of Git's version control capabilities. This allows for a detailed log of changes over time, making it easy to track the evolution of decisions.

4. **Transparent**: The use of GitHub Pages and the pull request workflow makes the decision-making process open and transparent. Team members and stakeholders can see the discussions and rationale behind each decision.

5. **Centralized**: Hosting the decision records on GitHub Pages ensures that all documentation is centralized and not scattered across various platforms. This makes it easier for team members to access and reference past decisions.

6. **Discoverable**: By making the decision records available on the open web, they become easily discoverable by anyone who needs to access them. This is particularly useful for new team members or external stakeholders who need to understand the architectural decisions made by the team.

In conclusion, the MADR format, combined with Jekyll for static site generation and GitHub for hosting and workflow, seems to effectively address all the criteria outlined in the problem statement. It provides a comprehensive solution for making architectural decisions in a collaborative, transparent, and organized manner.
While the MADR format with Jekyll and GitHub integration offers a structured approach to documenting architectural decisions, there are potential drawbacks and alternative considerations that might make it less ideal for some teams. Here are some reasons why MADR might not be the best option:

1. **Complexity and Learning Curve**: For teams not already familiar with Markdown, Jekyll, or GitHub, there could be a significant learning curve. This might slow down the decision-making process and require additional training or resources.

2. **Overhead**: The process of setting up and maintaining a Jekyll site on GitHub Pages, along with managing pull requests, could introduce additional overhead. This might be burdensome for smaller teams or those with limited technical resources.

3. **Accessibility**: While GitHub is a powerful tool, it may not be accessible or user-friendly for all team members, particularly those who are not developers. This could limit participation and hinder the broad-based collaboration that is desired.

4. **Security and Privacy Concerns**: Making architectural decisions publicly available on the open web might not be suitable for all organizations, especially those dealing with sensitive or proprietary information. This could pose security and privacy risks.

5. **Alternative Tools**: There are other tools and platforms that might better suit the needs of certain teams. For example, using a collaborative document platform like Confluence or Notion could provide a more user-friendly interface and integrate more seamlessly with existing workflows.

6. **Flexibility**: The structured nature of MADR might not accommodate all types of architectural decisions or the nuances of different projects. Some teams might benefit from a more flexible approach that allows for varied documentation styles.

In light of these considerations, it might be worth exploring alternative solutions that offer a balance between structure and flexibility, are more accessible to all team members, and align better with the team's existing tools and workflows. This could involve using a combination of collaborative platforms, version control systems, and documentation practices tailored to the specific needs and context of the team.

## Context and Problem Statement

A repository for each microservice or only one for all of them?

## Decision Drivers

* Development agility
* Microservices decoupling
* Big-picture scenario visibility

## Considered Options

* A repository for each microservice, and another for service definitions
* A single repository with decoupled microservices including service definitions
human_decision:
# Have a single repository for all microservices

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: A single repository for all microservices and service definitions this time, because this way its easy to get a big picture of the approach, for the purposes of this example.

In production, I'll consider separating microservices and service definitions on repos, in order to have separate triggers on CI for them.

I think [this approach](https://medium.com/namely-labs/how-we-build-grpc-services-at-namely-52a3ae9e7c35) will be a good fit for address this concern in production.

When deciding between having a repository for each microservice or a single repository for all microservices, it's important to weigh the decision drivers: development agility, microservices decoupling, and big-picture scenario visibility. Let's explore both options:

### Option 1: A Repository for Each Microservice, and Another for Service Definitions

**Pros:**
1. **Development Agility:** Each team can work independently on their microservice without being affected by changes in other services. This can lead to faster development cycles and more focused work.
2. **Microservices Decoupling:** This approach naturally enforces decoupling, as each microservice is isolated in its own repository. This can lead to better modularity and easier maintenance.
3. **Scalability:** As the number of microservices grows, this approach scales well because each service is managed independently.
4. **Version Control:** Each microservice can have its own versioning, allowing for more granular control over deployments and rollbacks.

**Cons:**
1. **Big-Picture Scenario Visibility:** It can be challenging to maintain a holistic view of the entire system since services are spread across multiple repositories.
2. **Complexity in Coordination:** Managing multiple repositories can increase the complexity of coordination, especially when changes affect multiple services.

### Option 2: A Single Repository with Decoupled Microservices Including Service Definitions

**Pros:**
1. **Big-Picture Scenario Visibility:** Having all microservices in a single repository can make it easier to see the overall architecture and how services interact with each other.
2. **Simplified Management:** With a single repository, there is less overhead in terms of repository management and coordination.
3. **Consistent Standards:** It can be easier to enforce coding standards and practices across all microservices when they are in a single repository.

**Cons:**
1. **Development Agility:** Changes in one microservice can potentially affect others, leading to slower development cycles and more complex merge conflicts.
2. **Microservices Decoupling:** While microservices can still be decoupled in a single repository, the physical separation is less clear, which might lead to tighter coupling over time.
3. **Scalability Issues:** As the number of microservices grows, a single repository can become unwieldy and harder to manage.

### Conclusion

The best option depends on the specific needs and context of the organization. If development agility and microservices decoupling are the top priorities, having a repository for each microservice is likely the better choice. This approach supports independent development and clear separation of concerns. However, if big-picture scenario visibility and simplified management are more critical, a single repository might be more suitable.

Ultimately, many organizations find a middle ground by using a hybrid approach: maintaining separate repositories for core microservices while using a single repository for shared components or service definitions. This can provide the benefits of both approaches while mitigating some of the downsides.
While I understand the reasoning behind advocating for separate repositories for each microservice, I would argue that a single repository with decoupled microservices, including service definitions, can be a more effective approach in many scenarios. Here's why:

### Option 2: A Single Repository with Decoupled Microservices Including Service Definitions

**Pros:**
1. **Big-Picture Scenario Visibility:** A single repository provides a comprehensive view of the entire system, making it easier to understand the interactions and dependencies between microservices. This can be crucial for architectural decisions and system-wide optimizations.
2. **Simplified Management:** Managing a single repository reduces the overhead associated with handling multiple repositories. This can streamline processes such as access control, repository setup, and integration with CI/CD pipelines.
3. **Consistent Standards and Practices:** With all microservices in one place, it's easier to enforce coding standards, testing practices, and other development guidelines across the board. This can lead to higher code quality and more uniform practices.
4. **Ease of Onboarding:** New developers can get up to speed more quickly when they have a single repository to explore, rather than navigating multiple repositories to understand the system.

**Cons:**
1. **Development Agility:** While there is a risk of changes in one microservice affecting others, this can be mitigated with proper branching strategies and CI/CD practices. Moreover, the benefits of having a unified view can outweigh the occasional merge conflict.
2. **Microservices Decoupling:** Although physical separation is less pronounced, logical decoupling can still be maintained through clear module boundaries and interface definitions. The repository structure can be organized to reflect this decoupling.
3. **Scalability Issues:** While a single repository can become large, modern version control systems and repository management tools are designed to handle such scenarios efficiently.

### Conclusion

In many cases, the benefits of a single repository—such as improved visibility, simplified management, and consistent practices—can outweigh the potential downsides. This approach can foster better collaboration and understanding across teams, leading to a more cohesive and well-integrated system.

However, it's important to note that the choice between a single repository and multiple repositories is not one-size-fits-all. Organizations should consider their specific context, team structure, and long-term goals when making this decision. In some cases, a hybrid approach, as mentioned earlier, might indeed be the best solution, combining the strengths of both strategies.

## Context and Problem Statement

As we have multiple services/applications deployed and monitored in the Operate First environment (ex. Jupyterhub, Argo, Superset, Observatorium, Project Thoth, AICoE CI pipelines etc), we need to implement an incident reporting setup for handling outages/incidents related to these services.

All the services are being monitored by [Prometheus](https://prometheus.io/). Prometheus scrapes and stores time series data identified by metric key/value pairs for each of the available services. These metrics can be used for measuring the service performance and alerting on any possible service degradation such as basic availability, latency, durability and any other applicable SLI/SLOs. These SLI/SLOs for the various services are defined and documented in the [SRE repository](https://github.com/operate-first/SRE/tree/master/sli-slo).

Alerting with Prometheus is separated into two parts. Alerting rules in Prometheus servers send alerts to an [Alertmanager](https://prometheus.io/docs/alerting/latest/alertmanager/). The Alertmanager then manages those alerts, including silencing, inhibition, aggregation and sending out notifications via methods such as email, on-call notification systems, and chat platforms.

Whether its a major bug, capacity issues, or an outage, users depending on the services expect an immediate response. Having an efficient incident management process is critical in ensuring that the incidents are always communicated to the users (via on-call notification systems) and handled by the team immediately. An on-call notification system is a system/software that provides an automated means of contacting users and communicating pertinent information during an incident. It also has additional on-call scheduling features that can be used to ensure that the right people on the team are available to address a problem during an incident. There are multiple on-call notification systems such as [PagerDuty](https://www.pagerduty.com/), [JIRA](https://www.atlassian.com/software/jira) etc that can be used for incident reporting, but which of these tools are best suitable for reporting the outages/incidents to our users?

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Visibility of incident reporting
  * How can the incidents be tracked and reported in an open and transparent manner for our users?
* Compatibility with Prometheus
  * Is the incident reporting tool compatible with Prometheus i.e. can it handle/receive Prometheus alerts?
* Complexity and cost of incident reporting tool
  * How easy/hard is it to manage and operate the incident reporting tool?
  * Is it a free or paid tool?
  * Is it open source?

## Considered Options

* Option 1:
  * Use [PagerDuty](https://www.pagerduty.com/) which is a popular paid on-call management and incident response platform
* Option 2:
  * Use open source tools like:
    * [Cabot](https://github.com/arachnys/cabot) - Python/Djano based monitoring platform
    * [OpenDuty](https://github.com/openduty/openduty) - Incident escalation tool similar to PagerDuty
    * [Dispatch](https://github.com/Netflix/dispatch) - Incident management tool by Netflix
    * [Response](https://github.com/monzo/response) - Django based incident management tool

  which are free, self-hosted infrastructure that provides some of the best features of PagerDuty, Pingdom etc without their cost and complexity
* Option 3:
  * Use [GitHub Alertmanager receiver](https://github.com/m-lab/alertmanager-github-receiver) which is a Prometheus Alertmanager webhook receiver that creates GitHub issues from alerts

# Alerting Setup for Operate First Monitoring

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: hemajv, 4n4nd, anishasthana, HumairAK, tumido, mhild

Technical Story: [issue-1](https://github.com/operate-first/SRE/issues/14), [issue-2](https://github.com/operate-first/SRE/issues/19), [issue-3](https://github.com/operate-first/blueprint/issues/16)

## Decision Outcome
Chosen option: **Option 3**, because:
  * The [GitHub alertmanager receiver](https://github.com/m-lab/alertmanager-github-receiver) can easily be configured and operated to function with Prometheus alerts. It automatically creates issues in GitHub repositories for any active alerts being fired, making it visible for any user to track
  * All communication/updates/concerns related to the incident can be easily handled by adding comments in the issues created by the GitHub receiver
  * Unlike Option 1, there is no additional cost involved
  * There is no requirement for using JIRA/Slack for incident tracking, which are the only supported options in some of the tools listed in Option 2 (such as [Dispatch](https://github.com/Netflix/dispatch) and [Response](https://github.com/monzo/response)) In any case that such a requirement surfaces, we can use GitHub bots for different platforms such as [GitHub for Slack](https://slack.github.com/) and [Google Chat](https://support.google.com/chat/answer/9632291?co=GENIE.Platform%3DAndroid&hl=en) to notify us of the issues immediately
  * It is actively being maintained and supported compared to some of the tools in Option 1 (such as [Cabot](https://github.com/arachnys/cabot) and [OpenDuty](https://github.com/openduty/openduty)) which lack community support
